Dmitri's staying on campus to start research with Professor Huang's lab - she actually followed through on the offer. Nico's plan is simply 'vibing at home' which he said with zero shame. Different approaches to downtime for different people. I'm just looking forward to sleeping in and not thinking about molecular structures for a few weeks.

One more Theo party to close out the semester. The energy was different tonight - lighter, more relaxed, the collective exhale of students who've finished their finals. Even the music seemed more celebratory than usual. People were actually dancing instead of standing around talking about how stressed they are. Theo was in his element as host, making sure everyone had drinks and introducing strangers to each other. End of semester magic is real. Tomorrow we scatter to different cities and families but tonight we're all here together.

Said proper goodbyes to the volleyball crew tonight and it was more emotional than expected. These people have become important to me over the course of the season - the practices, the games, the Sunrise Diner trips. Hana made us promise to stay active in the group chat over break. Reggie gave everyone bear hugs. We're already planning pickup games for when we get back. Sienna said she's looking forward to next season more than anything. The team bond doesn't end just because the competition did.

Sienna seemed happier tonight than she's been in weeks. The cloud that's been hanging over her appears to have lifted. I asked if she was okay and she actually smiled and said 'yeah, finally sorted some stuff out.' She didn't elaborate and I didn't push, but it was clear something had shifted for her. Whatever was weighing on her at the beginning of this month seems resolved. Good for her. Everyone deserves to end the semester on a positive note. I hope whatever she sorted stays sorted.

Packing to go home is its own special kind of chaos. How did I accumulate so much stuff in one semester? There are books I forgot I bought, clothes I forgot I owned, random items that have no clear origin story. Every surface of my room has something on it that needs to go in a bag or box. Kwame's equally overwhelmed, his photography equipment taking up half the packing space. We're both just standing in our disaster of a room wondering where to even start.

Kwame and I did our end-of-semester roommate check-in while packing, taking breaks to discuss how the living situation worked out. Verdict: pretty great actually. He's neat but not obsessive about it, respectful of my sleep schedule, doesn't bring drama into our shared space. I apparently meet his standards too because we confirmed we're rooming together again next semester without any hesitation. Finding a compatible roommate is honestly one of the luckier things that can happen in college. Kwame's good people.

The pigeon was at the window while I was packing, watching me disassemble my life for the semester. It felt like a final goodbye from my avian observer. We've had our silent relationship all semester - me trying to live my life, it judging me through the glass. I waved at it which is absurd because it's a bird and doesn't understand human gestures. But still. It felt like the right thing to do. See you next semester, weird surveillance pigeon. Try not to miss me too much.

Mira knocked on our door for final room inspections and she seemed different than usual. Less RA-robot-mode, more actual human. She asked how our finals went and actually listened to the answers. Made some light jokes about surviving another semester. It was nice seeing her be a person rather than a rule enforcer. Maybe it's the end-of-year relief softening everyone's edges, or maybe I just never gave her a chance to show her personality before. Either way, we parted on good terms. Goodbye Linford Hall, see you in January.

Home. My childhood room feels smaller than I remembered, or maybe I've just gotten used to college scale living. Everything is familiar but slightly off, like a place I know from dreams. Mom made my favorite dinner - her famous lasagna - and we sat together as a family for the first time in months. Dad asked about classes and actually seemed interested. The adjustment to being back in my parents' house instead of my dorm is going to take a few days. Different rules, different rhythms, but it's good to be here.

Grandma Lucia arrived at our house with enough food to sustain a small army through winter. She pinched my cheeks immediately upon seeing me - the woman cannot resist - and declared that I look 'too thin' which is her standard assessment regardless of my actual appearance. She could meet me after a month of eating only protein and she'd still say I need more meat on my bones. I love her relentlessly critical concern. She spent the next hour telling me about her various neighbor feuds and I listened to every word.

Cameron came over to catch up after months of only texting and occasional calls. His semester was rough too - calculus defeated him as predicted, but he's going to retake it. We fell back into our easy friendship like no time had passed. At one point he looked at me and said 'You actually made friends at college. Like, real friends.' He sounded almost proud. Then he said 'I'm proud of you' and it hit me how much I've changed since leaving home. Growth is strange when someone else points it out.

Texted Sienna to check in on how she's doing over break. We've been messaging occasionally, maintaining the volleyball team bond. She mentioned in passing that she's been dealing with ex drama - apparently he works at some campus coffee place and it's made things complicated. I felt my brain start connecting dots. Campus coffee place. Ex drama. Complicated. But there are multiple coffee places on campus, I reminded myself. Could be anywhere. Still, something about the detail stuck with me.

Wait. Campus coffee place. The Grind is a campus coffee place. Frankie has been dealing with ex drama all semester. Frankie works at The Grind. Sienna's ex works at a campus coffee place. The timeline matches up. The mood shifts, the tension Sienna's been carrying, Frankie's struggles earlier in the semester. Are Sienna and Frankie's exes... the same person? Are THEY each other's exes? My brain is spiraling now. This might be nothing but it also might be a massive coincidence or a massive connection.

Grades finally posted and I'm almost afraid to look. Deep breath. Click. ORGANIC CHEMISTRY: B+. The same grade as my midterm. Consistent improvement? More like consistent survival. But honestly, a B+ in Huang's orgo class feels like a victory. I worked so hard for this. All those study sessions, all those office hours with Ronan, all that stress. It paid off in a solid grade I can be proud of. Not perfect, but proof that I can handle this level of academic challenge. I'll take it.

Group chat exploded when grades dropped. Everyone sharing their results. Beatrice: A+, because the universe would collapse if Beatrice got anything less than perfection. Dmitri: also A+, validating Professor Huang's interest in recruiting him for research. Nico: B-, which he's thrilled about because he genuinely thought he might fail. We all made it. Every single person in our study group passed orgo with decent grades. The system worked. We supported each other through academic hell and came out the other side intact.

Got a message from Mr. Odom, my high school chemistry teacher. He said he heard through the grapevine that I was doing well in organic chemistry at college. Word apparently travels fast in small towns. He was my first real science inspiration, the teacher who made me think I might actually be good at this stuff. Knowing that he knows I'm succeeding feels meaningful. I sent back a thank you for setting me on this path. Teachers rarely get to see how their students turn out. I wanted him to know he mattered.

Christmas morning at home and the house is chaos in the best way. Too much food everywhere, too many relatives packed into too small a space, the same holiday songs playing on repeat. Grandma Lucia is commanding the kitchen like a general directing troops. Dad and my uncles are arguing about football in the living room. Mom is trying to coordinate the gift exchange while also preventing kitchen disasters. It's overwhelming and loud and absolutely perfect. This is what the holidays are supposed to feel like.

Grandma Lucia pulled me aside during Christmas dinner and pressed a hundred dollars into my hand 'for books' she said, with this knowing wink that made clear she expected me to spend it on whatever I wanted. That's our tradition - she gives me money with a practical excuse and I accept it without questioning. The wink is the acknowledgment that we both know the truth. She hugged me tight and told me to study hard but not too hard. Grandma wisdom. I'm going to miss her when I go back to school.

New Year's Eve at Cameron's house party with a mix of high school friends and people I vaguely recognize from around town. When midnight hit, I found myself in a strange position: cheering with people from my past while texting people from my present. Sent happy new year messages to the volleyball group chat, to the study group, to Zara and Ivy. Two worlds I'm straddling now. Home friends and college friends. Both feel real, both feel important. The new year holds both versions of my life.

Back on campus for the spring semester and everything feels both familiar and fresh. Linford Hall smells exactly the same - that specific combination of floor cleaner and student living. Kwame's already here when I arrive, his photography equipment spread across every surface like he never left. Some things don't change. We hugged like people who genuinely missed each other, which I guess we did. It's good to be back. Home was nice but this dorm room has become my other home now.

Registered for spring classes and my schedule is intense. Orgo 2, the sequel nobody asked for but everyone pre-med has to endure. Also signed up for stats which I've heard is manageable, and some film gen ed for balance because I need at least one class that doesn't make me want to cry. The film class is about classical Hollywood cinema which sounds genuinely interesting. Balance is important. Can't be stressed about chemistry every waking moment. Need some space for movies and easier expectations.

THE PIGEON IS BACK. Same window. Same ledge. Same unsettling stare directly into my room. It's like it was waiting for me all break, maintaining its post, ready to resume surveillance operations the moment I returned. I actually laughed when I saw it this morning. Some things in life are uncertain but pigeon vigilance is constant. My weird avian companion has become a tradition at this point. Welcome back to me, I guess. The semester officially begins when the pigeon resumes its watch.

First shift back at The Grind and it felt like coming home to a second family. Oscar greeted me with his version of warmth, which is basically just being slightly less grumpy than usual. He showed me pictures of his niece from the holidays, which was surprisingly personal for Oscar. He doesn't usually share family stuff. The shop looks the same, smells the same, has the same steady stream of caffeine-desperate students. I slipped back into the rhythm of making drinks like I never left. Good to be back behind the counter.

VICTOR! That's the name of the regular customer I could never remember. He came in today, same time as always, same order - chai latte with his particular specifications. But this time he was chatty, told me his dissertation defense is scheduled for March and he's nervous but prepared. His research is on medieval trade routes, which he explained with such enthusiasm that I actually found myself interested in commerce from centuries ago. Dr. Victor (soon) is a good guy. I'm rooting for his defense.

First day of Orgo 2 and Professor Huang wasted no time establishing the stakes. She walked in, wrote 'CARBONYL CHEMISTRY' in large letters on the board, turned to face us, and said simply 'This will be your life now.' No greeting, no syllabus review, just an immediate declaration that we're in for serious work. Classic Huang. The students who survived Orgo 1 exchanged knowing looks. We know what we're in for. The battle continues. At least this time we know our enemy.

Study group reunion! Same people, same mission, new semester of chemistry to survive. Beatrice arrived with new supplies, including a 48-pack of highlighters she got for Christmas. Forty-eight colors of organization. Dmitri looked tan somehow, apparently his family visited Florida over break. Nico was late as usual. We fell back into our rhythm immediately - Beatrice explaining, Dmitri confirming, Nico questioning, me noting. The system works so we're not changing it. Round two of organic chemistry begins with the squad intact.

The volleyball group chat has been active since we got back. Hana is already talking about spring intramural signups, analyzing potential competition, discussing what we need to work on to come back stronger than last semester. The championship loss is clearly still motivating her. She's channeling the disappointment into preparation. Messages about practice schedules and conditioning exercises have been flowing. Our captain's leadership doesn't take breaks. If anything, the time off made her more determined.

Sienna confirmed she's playing again this semester which is great news for the team. We need her setting abilities if we want to compete for another championship. More importantly, she seemed genuinely enthusiastic in her messages. The heaviness that was weighing on her last semester appears to have lifted over break. She's engaging in the group chat with the same energy she had at the start of last season, before whatever was bothering her started affecting her mood. Good to have full Sienna back.

Stats class might actually kill me. The professor teaches like everyone already has a foundational understanding of statistical concepts, which I very much do not. He throws around terminology without explanation and moves through slides faster than I can take notes. I spent the entire first lecture lost, trying to catch up to a conversation that started before I arrived. This is going to require significant outside studying just to keep pace. So much for stats being the 'easy' quantitative course. Nothing is easy.

Film class is the complete opposite of stats - super chill professor who spent the entire first day just talking about his favorite movies and why cinema matters. No syllabus stress, no immediate assignments, just enthusiasm for the art form. We're starting with Hitchcock, which I'm actually excited about. The professor's energy is contagious. He clearly loves what he teaches and wants us to love it too. This is going to be my sanctuary class, the one that reminds me learning can be enjoyable.

Mira did her start-of-semester rounds tonight, going door to door checking that everyone made it back okay. But something was different about her approach this time. She was almost friendly, asking genuine questions about our breaks rather than just going through the motions. Maybe the end-of-last-semester thaw wasn't temporary. Kwame froze the moment she knocked - his instinctive paranoia kicking in - but relaxed when he realized she wasn't there to enforce anything. Progress in RA-resident relations.

Mira lingered at our door after the official check-in stuff was done, specifically talking to Kwame about his photography project. She asked detailed questions about his techniques, his artistic influences, seemed genuinely interested in his work. Kwame was animated in a way he usually isn't around authority figures, excited to have someone ask about his passion. I watched the exchange with curiosity. There was something in the dynamic that felt different from just RA-to-resident politeness. Interesting development to observe.

Zara and Andre have settled into established couple status remarkably fast. They're that pair now who shows up to things together, finishes each other's sentences, has standing date nights. It's cute in a way that would be annoying if I didn't genuinely like both of them. Zara seems happy in a more stable way than she ever was with Malik. Andre grounds her. They complement each other well - his calm intellectualism balancing her passionate intensity. Sometimes the rebound relationship turns out to be the real thing.

Work was slow enough today that Oscar had me help with inventory, counting supplies and checking expiration dates in the storage area. He's incredibly particular about organization - everything has a specific place, specific labels, specific systems. At first it seemed excessive but he explained the reasoning behind each decision and it actually makes sense. A well-organized back area means faster service during rush periods. His attention to detail is why The Grind runs smoothly. I learned more about coffee shop management than expected.

Margaret, our favorite regular customer, asked me today if I had a 'nice young person' in my life. The grandma energy was strong. She said it with such genuine caring curiosity, like she wants to see me happy and settled. I told her I was focused on school for now and she nodded approvingly but also gave me this look that said she'd continue asking periodically. She's basically adopted me as a surrogate grandson. Between her and my actual grandmother, I have plenty of elderly women invested in my romantic future.

Concerning development: a new coffee shop opened directly across the street from The Grind. Very sleek, very modern, clearly targeting the same student demographic we serve. Oscar was visibly stressed about the competition, kept glancing out the window at their storefront throughout the day. I tried to reassure him that loyal customers won't abandon us for something shiny and new, but the worry was written on his face. The Grind has been here forever. It would be devastating to watch it struggle against a newcomer.

Kwame and I decided to scope out the competition by visiting the new coffee shop together. The aesthetic is extremely Instagram-worthy - exposed brick, designer furniture, that carefully curated industrial vibe. Very photogenic. But here's the thing: the actual coffee was just fine. Overpriced for the quality, honestly. The latte was seventy percent foam and the barista seemed more interested in the tip jar than the customer experience. The Grind has nothing to worry about. Substance will beat style in the long run.

Hana sent out the official volleyball practice schedule for the semester and it's intense. Multiple sessions per week starting immediately, no easing back into things. She's made it clear that last semester's championship loss is fuel for this year's fire. We're going to be better conditioned, better coordinated, better prepared than any team we face. Her message included phrases like 'redemption arc' and 'unfinished business.' Captain Hana is in full competitive mode. We follow her lead.

Ronan is holding office hours again this semester and I've already put them in my calendar as recurring appointments. That man is a saint for continuing to help undergrads while managing his own graduate research. His explanations are consistently clearer than the lectures, his patience is endless, and he genuinely seems to want us to succeed rather than just surviving his TA duties. I'm going to need him even more for Orgo 2 than I did for Orgo 1. Thank goodness he's still around.

Tobias and Celeste had another fight tonight. I could hear it through the paper-thin walls of Linford Hall, their voices rising and falling in that familiar pattern of couple disagreement. This is at least the second major argument I've witnessed since moving in. They always seem to resolve things and go back to being an adorable couple, but the frequency of these blowups makes me wonder about the underlying stability. Relationships from the outside only show you what people want you to see. The walls reveal more truth.

Saw Kwame and Mira talking in the common room tonight and the vibe was... interesting. They were actually talking - not RA-to-resident functional communication, but real conversation. Body language relaxed, leaning toward each other, Mira laughing at something Kwame said. I've never seen her laugh before, not genuinely. It transformed her whole face. I watched from a distance, not wanting to interrupt whatever was developing. Something is definitely happening between my roommate and our RA. The plot thickens.

Asked Kwame about the Mira situation when he got back to our room and he immediately got defensive. 'We were just talking about photography,' he said, not making eye contact. His voice did that thing it does when he's not being entirely truthful. I didn't push - if something's happening, he'll tell me when he's ready. But 'just talking about photography' doesn't explain the body language I observed, or the way his ears got red when I asked. Kwame has never been good at hiding things. This will unfold.

First volleyball practice of the semester and my legs are already screaming in protest. Everyone's a little rusty from the break, timing slightly off, conditioning clearly degraded. But the energy was good despite the physical struggles. Hana ran us through fundamentals, getting back to basics before building up to more complex plays. It's smart coaching strategy. Even Sienna, who's usually flawless, looked like she was finding her rhythm again. We'll be back to form soon. The rust will shake off.

After practice, Sienna mentioned that volleyball is a good distraction from 'life stuff' she's dealing with. She said it casually, like it was no big deal, but I noticed. She's still processing something even if she seems better than last semester. I told her I'm here if she ever wants to talk and she smiled and said she appreciates it. The team really has become a support system beyond just athletics. We look out for each other. Whatever Sienna's dealing with, she's not dealing with it alone.

Juno's back at work! She showed up for her shift looking better than she did before break, more grounded and present. When I asked how she was, she actually seemed to mean it when she said 'good.' The time away from school helped apparently. She talked about spending break reflecting on whether this was the right place for her and deciding to give it another real try. Her whole energy is different - less overwhelmed, more intentional. I hope this positive shift sticks. She deserves to enjoy her college experience.

Grandma Lucia's Sunday call hit all the usual notes: am I eating enough (asked three times), am I sleeping enough (asked twice), do I have a girlfriend yet (asked once but emphatically). She's already planning what to send me for Valentine's Day, which is still weeks away but Grandma prepares early. I suspect another cookie delivery is incoming. She wants to know about my classes, my friends, every detail of my life. Talking to her grounds me, reminds me where I come from and who's rooting for me.

Dmitri helped me with stats concepts after study group tonight and I'm convinced his brain operates on a different level than the rest of us. He sees patterns in mathematical relationships that are completely invisible to me. What takes me an hour to understand he grasps immediately, then patiently explains it multiple ways until it clicks. His math brain is genuinely unfair in the best possible way. Between him for stats and Ronan for orgo, I have access to the tutoring resources I desperately need to survive.

Cameron texted asking when he can come visit campus. He's been talking about it since the road trip discussions over winter break - wants to see where I live, meet my friends, experience college life vicariously through me since his school experience is apparently lacking. We're thinking maybe spring break would work. It would be fun to show him around, introduce him to everyone, let him experience a Theo party. Merging my home life friend with my college life feels like an important milestone somehow.

INCREDIBLE NEWS: The pastry detective show got renewed for another season! Kwame and I saw the announcement together and celebrated like we'd won something personally. Chef Inspector Moreau lives on to solve more baking-related crimes! The show is objectively ridiculous but our commitment to it has become a cornerstone of our roommate relationship. Tuesday nights watching the pastry detective are sacred. We immediately texted each other speculation about what crimes the new season might involve. Croissant heists? Soufflé sabotage? The possibilities are endless.

First game of the new season against Thornton Hall and we won easily. Like, dominant performance from start to finish. The rust from early practices has completely shaken off. Sienna's sets were back to their elite level, finding hitters perfectly, running the offense like a conductor leading an orchestra. The chemistry we built last season didn't disappear over break - if anything, the time apart made us appreciate playing together more. Strong start to the redemption tour. Championship or nothing this time.

Sunrise Diner tradition continues after our victory. The waitress recognized us the moment we walked in - we've become regulars through our post-game rituals. She already knew some of our usual orders. There's something nice about being known somewhere, being part of the regular fabric of a place. We took over our usual corner, celebrated the win, talked about what's working and what we need to improve. These diner sessions are as important to our team chemistry as actual practices. Pancakes and volleyball analysis.

Caught Kwame leaving Mira's room at 11pm tonight. He tried to act casual when he saw me in the hallway, like there was nothing unusual about exiting the RA's private room late in the evening. 'I was helping her with something,' he said, not elaborating on what that something might be. I raised an eyebrow but didn't press. The evidence is mounting. My roommate and our strict RA have something going on that extends beyond photography conversations and rule enforcement.

Confronted Kwame directly when he got back to our room. Told him I'm not stupid, I can see what's happening with Mira. He sighed and finally admitted they've been 'hanging out.' I asked if that meant dating and he said 'it's complicated' in a way that definitely means yes but with caveats. The RA-resident dynamic makes things weird, there are rules about this stuff probably, and they're trying to figure out how to navigate it. My roommate is dating the authority figure. This is a plot twist I didn't see coming.

The irony of Kwame dating Mira is absolutely delicious. This is the same RA who wrote him up at the beginning of the semester, who made him paranoid about his speaker, who he used to hide things from the moment he heard her knock. And now he's sneaking into her room for late night hangouts. Life comes at you fast. I promised to keep it quiet since they're still figuring things out, but I reserve the right to tease him mercilessly in private. The comedy writes itself.

Oscar was in full crisis mode at work today because the espresso machine broke down again. This machine has been temperamental all year and today it finally gave up entirely during what should have been a normal morning rush. The repair guy can't come until Thursday, which means we're operating at significantly reduced capacity for days. Oscar called Frankie, who came in on his day off to help manage the chaos. Everyone was scrambling. The Grind without espresso is like a library without books - technically still there but missing the point.

Frankie kept everything running smoothly at work today despite the espresso machine crisis. He managed the backup equipment, kept customers calm about longer wait times, even made jokes to ease the tension. Oscar called him 'indispensable' which coming from Oscar is basically a medal of honor. Watching Frankie handle chaos with such competence made me realize how much he's grown into a leader at The Grind. He's not just a coworker anymore, he's essential to how the place functions. Oscar knows it too.

Victor the chai latte grad student was extra chatty today while I made his drink. He told me random facts about the spice trade - did you know cinnamon was once worth more than gold? - all connecting back to his dissertation on medieval trade routes. The passion he has for centuries-old commerce is genuinely infectious. I found myself actually interested in how spices moved across continents and shaped economies. His defense is coming up in March and I'm weirdly invested in his success. Go Dr. Victor.

Frankie was in a weird mood at work, kept checking his phone and looking distracted. During our break he mentioned that someone from his past keeps trying to reach out - not his ex specifically but someone connected to that whole situation. He didn't elaborate but it's clearly weighing on him. Just when he seemed to be moving on, the past keeps finding ways to resurface. I told him I'm here if he needs to talk. Sometimes people just need to know someone's in their corner.

Mira joined us for dinner in the dining hall tonight and it was surreal. Like, actually sat down at our table as a person rather than hovering as an authority figure. She ate cafeteria food with us, made jokes, complained about her classes like any other student. The RA persona was completely absent. It's strange reconciling this version of Mira with the one who knocked on doors enforcing quiet hours. Kwame was glowing the entire meal, clearly happy to have her in his world publicly.

Mira is actually cool when she's not in official mode. She's got opinions about movies, strong feelings about campus food quality, interesting perspectives from her public health studies. Kwame was showing her something on his phone and they both laughed at whatever it was. Watching them interact without the RA-resident barrier is nice. They seem genuinely good together, complementary in ways I wouldn't have predicted. Kwame tends toward chaos and Mira brings structure. Balance.

Volleyball practice was brutal tonight. Coach Tran made us run drills until we couldn't feel our legs, then made us run more. It's like he's trying to forge us into machines through sheer physical exhaustion. My calves are cramping as I write this. But underneath the pain, I understand the strategy - we need to be in better shape than any team we face so we can outlast them in long matches. Conditioning wins championships when skill is equal. Still hurts though.

Sienna stayed after practice to work on timing with me, which has become our unofficial routine. She mentioned she's been sleeping better lately, said she's 'finally dealing with stuff instead of avoiding it.' Progress. Whatever was weighing on her seems to be lifting through intentional effort rather than just time. I'm glad she's addressing it directly. She looked lighter, more present during our drills. The heaviness in her energy is dissipating. Healing isn't linear but she's clearly moving forward.

Orgo exam next week and the anxiety is building. Beatrice has already scheduled four separate study sessions between now and test day, each with a specific focus area. Her organizational intensity used to intimidate me but now I find it comforting. Having a structured plan makes the overwhelming feel manageable. Carbonyl chemistry is dense material and I need all the preparation time I can get. Going to rely heavily on Ronan's office hours this week too. All hands on deck.

Dmitri played the chess guy in the quad today and LOST. I was shocked because Dmitri is genuinely good at chess, plays competitively and everything. But Howard (I finally learned the old man's name) just dismantled him methodically. What was amazing was Dmitri's reaction: instead of being frustrated, he seemed intrigued. 'I will return,' he said ominously as he shook Howard's hand. A rivalry has been born. The quad chess showdowns are going to be appointment viewing from now on.

Grandma Lucia's Valentine's package arrived early because she plans ahead for everything. Heart-shaped cookies, a card with a fifty dollar bill inside, and a handwritten note saying 'find someone nice.' The woman is relentless about my romantic life but I can't be mad when the pressure comes wrapped in baked goods and cash. I called to thank her and she spent twenty minutes asking follow-up questions about whether I'd met anyone. Grandma's love language is equal parts cookies and interrogation.

Brought Grandma's Valentine's cookies to work to share and they were an immediate hit. Oscar ate three, which is unprecedented - the man usually avoids sugar. He said with complete sincerity that my grandmother is a treasure and asked if she'd ever consider selling her recipes. I told him she guards them like state secrets. Frankie, Juno, even the customers who got samples were raving. Grandma Lucia has fans across the campus coffee shop ecosystem now. Her baking legacy expands.

There's a girl in my stats class who always sits next to me, like specifically chooses the seat beside mine even when other spots are available. We've developed this silent rapport over the semester - sympathetic glances when the professor says something confusing, shared eye rolls at particularly dense material. Today she actually passed me her notes from a section I missed. No words exchanged, just a helpful gesture between two people struggling through the same academic challenge together.

Finally learned the stats girl's name: Priya. Data science major, which explains why she actually understands the statistical concepts that make my brain hurt. We ended up talking after class about the upcoming exam and she offered to study together sometime. I accepted immediately because I need all the help I can get and also because she seems genuinely nice. Making friends in classes outside my usual circles is good for expanding my world. Priya might be my stats survival partner.

Kwame officially told me that he and Mira are 'seeing each other' as if I hadn't already figured that out weeks ago. I appreciated the formal acknowledgment though. He wanted me to know directly rather than just letting me piece it together from observations. I acted appropriately surprised, didn't mention the late night hallway sighting or the obvious body language I've been noticing. Let him have his moment of revelation. Being a good roommate sometimes means pretending you don't already know things.

Kwame's worried about what people will think about him dating an RA on her own floor. There are probably policies about this kind of thing, potential conflicts of interest, the perception of favoritism. They're keeping it quiet for now while they figure out how to navigate the situation properly. Mira might need to request a floor transfer or something to make it officially okay. I promised discretion. The complications of their relationship are theirs to manage. I'm just supportive background noise.

Game against Reynolds today was tough but we pulled through with a win. The other team was scrappy, made us work for every point. At one point someone in the crowd said they recognized Sienna, which seemed to throw her off momentarily. A random comment from a stranger having that effect was strange. She recovered quickly and played well the rest of the match, but I noticed the flicker of something cross her face when they said it.

After the game, the person who recognized Sienna came up and asked 'You dated that barista guy right?' Sienna's face went through several emotions in rapid succession before she changed the subject smoothly. But I saw it - the brief flash of discomfort, the forced casual deflection. Barista guy. Someone who works at a coffee place. The dots are connecting in my head whether I want them to or not. This can't be coincidence anymore.

She mentioned last semester that her ex works at 'some campus coffee place.' The Grind is a campus coffee place. Frankie has been dealing with ex drama all year. The timelines match, the emotional patterns match, everything matches. My volleyball teammate and my coworker used to date each other. They're the exes haunting each other's stories. I'm connected to both sides of a breakup I knew nothing about. This is either a massive coincidence or the campus is even smaller than I thought.

Went to Zara's birthday party at Andre's apartment tonight. His place is very him - walls lined with books, philosophical quotes framed on the wall, a poster of Nietzsche that I think is meant to be ironic but with Andre you can never be sure. The vibe was chill and intellectual, lots of conversations about ideas rather than gossip. Zara seemed happy surrounded by people who care about her. Good birthday energy. Andre clearly put thought into making it special for her.

Orgo exam done and I think it went okay. Professor Patel is tough but fair - the questions were hard but they were the kind of hard we prepared for, not gotcha tricks designed to destroy confidence. I walked out feeling cautiously optimistic rather than shell-shocked, which is a significant upgrade from last semester's Huang exams. The study group work paid off. Beatrice's color-coded preparation guides continue to be invaluable. Now we wait for results and try not to spiral.

Research showcase tomorrow and Layla and I did one final run-through of our presentation tonight. Our sleep deprivation and memory project has come together really well - clear hypothesis, solid methodology, interesting findings. Layla handles the public speaking portions better than me but I've gotten more comfortable with my sections through practice. We make a good team. Tomorrow we present our work to professors and peers. Nervous but prepared. This is what all those hours of research led to.

THE SHOWCASE WENT GREAT! Our presentation on sleep deprivation and memory was well-received. People asked thoughtful questions and we had good answers prepared. The best moment was when a professor in the audience gave us her card afterward, said she was impressed by our methodology and wanted to discuss potential next steps. Layla nearly fainted from excitement - that kind of faculty interest is exactly what she's been hoping for. We did it. Our research actually mattered to someone beyond just getting a grade.

Valentine's Day shift at The Grind was absolute chaos. Everyone wanted heart-shaped foam on their lattes, couples were ordering for each other with elaborate specifications, the line never got shorter. I made more romantic-themed drinks in one shift than I've made all semester. At one point, a couple literally broke up AT the counter while ordering, which was incredibly uncomfortable for everyone involved. Love is complicated. Coffee is simple. I prefer making the coffee. What's a good way to decompress after a stressful shift like this?

Kwame and Mira did something together for Valentine's Day tonight, though he was coy about the details. He came back to the dorm looking genuinely happy, the kind of relaxed contentment that comes from spending quality time with someone you care about. I didn't pry for specifics - he'll share if he wants to. It's nice seeing him in a good relationship, even if the RA complication makes it complicated. Everyone deserves to have someone who makes them smile like that.

Orgo exam results came back: B. Not B+, just B. A step down from last semester's consistency. Professor Huang wrote 'room for improvement' which feels pointed but fair. I'm disappointed but trying to keep perspective - a B is still passing, still decent, just not what I've come to expect from myself. Need to figure out where I went wrong and adjust for the next exam. Can't let one setback derail the momentum I've built. What are some effective strategies for bouncing back after a disappointing exam grade?

Sienna was distracted at practice today, her timing slightly off, her focus elsewhere. When I asked what was going on, she said she's 'running into someone tomorrow I'd rather avoid.' The way she said it carried weight - this wasn't about a random acquaintance, it was about someone significant. She didn't elaborate and I didn't push, but I could see the stress in her shoulders, the way she was bracing herself for an uncomfortable encounter. I hope whatever tomorrow brings, she handles it okay.

I have work in the morning. Sienna mentioned once that she usually avoids coming by The Grind. At the time I didn't think much of it, figured she just preferred other coffee spots. But now, with everything I've pieced together, the avoidance makes sense. If Frankie is her ex, of course she'd avoid his workplace. The coffee shop where I work is a place she deliberately stays away from. Tomorrow she's apparently facing someone she doesn't want to see. Is it tomorrow at The Grind?

I'm connecting all the dots now and the picture is undeniable. Frankie's ex 'ended badly' and 'keeps reaching out.' Sienna's ex works at a campus coffee place and she avoids The Grind. Frankie was struggling emotionally early in the semester. Sienna was carrying something heavy that affected her game. The timelines overlap. The emotional patterns mirror each other. They were together and then they weren't, and I've been friends with both sides of this breakup without knowing it. The campus really is that small.

It happened. Sienna came into The Grind this morning. Frankie was working. I watched from behind the counter as they saw each other, the recognition passing between them like an electric current. The ENERGY in that interaction was palpable - years of history compressed into a moment of eye contact across a coffee shop. Everyone in the vicinity could feel something significant happening even if they didn't know what. My two separate friend groups just collided in real time.

They were polite to each other. Too polite. The performative courtesy of people who once meant everything to each other and now have to pretend they're acquaintances. 'Hi, how are you' exchanged with perfect neutrality, meaning nothing and everything simultaneously. Sienna got her drink and left quickly, didn't linger, didn't make it weird. Frankie was quiet for the rest of the shift, processing the encounter internally. I pretended I didn't notice the weight of what I'd just witnessed.

So my coworker and my volleyball teammate used to date and I'm apparently the connection point between their separate post-breakup lives. This is fine. Everything is fine. I'm going to pretend I don't know what I know, treat both of them normally, and not let this information change how I interact with either friend group. It's none of my business really, their history is their history. I just happen to exist in both of their present lives. Nothing weird about that at all.

The pigeon brought a friend again. Second pigeon era has begun. There are now two birds on my window ledge every morning, conducting what I can only assume is surveillance on my daily activities. The original pigeon apparently recruited a partner for this important work. Doubled the observation capacity. I'm choosing to interpret this as the pigeon trusting me enough to introduce me to its companion rather than a coordinated intelligence operation. Either way, my mornings now include two judgmental birds.

Crawford rematch day. This is the team that beat us in the championship last semester, the two-point loss that still stings. Hana's pregame speech was intense - she talked about redemption, about proving we're the better team, about not letting last season's ending define us. Her words hit different because we all feel the unfinished business. We want this win not just for the record but for ourselves. Time to show Crawford that their championship was a fluke.

WE WON. REVENGE AGAINST CRAWFORD. Three sets, dominated the third, never let them back in it after we got momentum. Everything we've been working toward clicked perfectly in this game. Their captain looked devastated at the end, probably feeling what we felt when they beat us for the championship. Karma is real and it wears volleyball shorts. This might be the most satisfying win of my athletic life. We proved something to ourselves tonight.

The celebration after beating Crawford was euphoric. Hana actually lifted Sienna in the air during the post-game team huddle, a spontaneous explosion of joy. Reggie was screaming so loud I thought he'd lose his voice. I might have cried a little - not full tears, just the watery eyes that come when emotions overflow. This team has been through so much together. Losing last semester, grinding through practices, building back stronger. This win validated all of it.

Sienna was in the best mood I've seen from her all year during the post-game celebration. She said beating Crawford felt like 'closure on a lot of things' which I think meant more than just volleyball. The ex situation, the emotional weight she's been carrying, all of it seemed lighter in that moment of victory. Sports can do that sometimes - give you a container for feelings that don't have anywhere else to go. She looked free. Whatever she's been processing, this win helped.

Kwame snuck Mira into the post-game party at Theo's and they thought they were being discreet about it. They were absolutely not being discreet about it. Arriving together, standing close all night, sharing looks that screamed 'we're together.' Multiple people asked me what was going on between them and I played dumb as promised. But the secret is barely a secret anymore. They might as well just make it official since everyone has basically figured it out anyway.

VICTOR PASSED HIS DISSERTATION DEFENSE! He came into The Grind looking like a completely different person - relaxed, unburdened, practically glowing with relief. Months of stress about his medieval trade routes research, all the preparation and anxiety, and he did it. We gave him his chai latte for free and Oscar made a little sign that said 'Congratulations Dr. Victor!' He seemed genuinely touched. The regular customers become like extended family. I'm so happy for him. Years of work validated.

Oscar's handmade 'Congratulations Dr. Victor' sign is still taped to the espresso machine days later. I think he's more sentimental than he lets on. Victor has been coming to The Grind for years apparently, long before I started working here. Watching a regular customer achieve a major life milestone like finishing a PhD feels meaningful somehow. The Grind isn't just a coffee shop, it's a community space where people's lives unfold. Oscar understands that even if he'd never say it out loud.

Kwame and Mira are officially telling people about their relationship now. The secret-keeping period is over. She's getting transferred to a different floor next semester to avoid any conflict of interest issues with being his RA. They handled it properly, went through the appropriate channels, made it official and above-board. I'm glad they don't have to sneak around anymore. Relationships are hard enough without adding institutional complications on top of personal ones.

Tobias won't stop joking about Kwame 'dating the enemy' - the RA who once wrote him up now being his girlfriend. He thinks it's hilarious and honestly it kind of is from an outside perspective. The narrative arc is pretty funny. Mira takes the teasing in stride, even makes jokes back about how Kwame was her 'problem resident' before becoming her boyfriend. Their origin story includes a noise complaint. Romantic comedy material right there.

Stats exam tomorrow and Priya and I crammed for three hours together tonight. Her explanations are somehow clearer than the professor's - she breaks down statistical concepts into logical steps that actually make sense to my non-math brain. Having a study partner who genuinely understands the material is invaluable. I feel more prepared than I expected to feel. Still nervous, but the 'I have no idea what's happening' panic has been replaced with cautious confidence. Can you explain the difference between Type I and Type II errors one more time? I want to make sure I've got it.

Stats exam done and I think it went well. The concepts that confused me earlier in the semester clicked when I needed them to. Priya finished before me and waited outside to compare answers - she said she 'crushed it' with her usual confidence. I believe her. My performance was probably more modest but I'm hopeful for a decent grade. Having a strong study partner made all the difference. Solo studying wasn't cutting it for this material. Collaboration saved me.

Cameron arrives tomorrow for his campus visit and I realized I should probably clean my half of the room. There's a pile of laundry that's been growing for weeks, textbooks scattered everywhere, general chaos that I've become blind to through daily exposure. Can't have my hometown friend thinking I've become a complete slob at college. Spent an hour making the space presentable. Kwame watched me clean with amusement since he knows I never put this much effort in for normal days.

CAMERON IS HERE! Picked him up from the bus station and immediately started the campus tour. He kept saying 'this is so college' at everything - the old buildings, the quad, the students sitting on lawns with laptops. Like he was experiencing the platonic ideal of university life through fresh eyes. It was fun seeing my everyday environment through his perspective. Things I've stopped noticing became interesting again when I had to explain them to someone new.

Introduced Cameron to Kwame and they immediately bonded over some video game I've never heard of. Within twenty minutes they were deep in conversation about strategies and updates and characters, completely excluding me from the discussion. I should probably be annoyed but honestly it's nice that my college roommate and my hometown best friend get along. Different parts of my life connecting positively. Kwame invited Cameron to stay for our pastry detective viewing night.

Took Cameron to The Grind to show him where I work. Oscar was in his element, friendly in his gruff way, asking Cameron questions about his visit. Frankie made Cameron a fancy latte with impressive foam art. Juno thought Cameron was cute, which she mentioned to me not at all subtly after he stepped away to take a photo. The whole staff seemed invested in making a good impression on my friend. The Grind family welcoming a visitor from my other life.

Party at Theo's with Cameron and he fit right in with my college friend group. Within an hour he was having intense conversations with multiple people, charming everyone with his particular brand of earnest enthusiasm. At one point I saw him and Ivy engaged in what looked like a heated debate, both gesturing emphatically. Cameron making Ivy argue loudly is actually impressive - she usually saves that energy for formal academic settings. He passed the friend integration test.

Cameron's verdict on my college friends: 'Actually cool, not just weird.' Coming from him, that's a compliment. He was worried before visiting that I'd surrounded myself with pretentious intellectuals or something, but the reality is just normal people figuring out their lives. He said he could see why I'm happy here. That validation from someone who's known me forever means something. Home life and college life can coexist. They don't have to be separate worlds.

Took Cameron to watch volleyball practice so he could see the athletic side of my college life. Hana was in full captain mode, running drills with intensity, and Cameron was impressed by the level of organization. 'This is more serious than I expected for intramural,' he said. He's not wrong - Hana approaches volleyball like it's her profession. The team looked good with an audience, everyone playing a bit sharper knowing we had a spectator.

Sienna talked to Cameron for a bit after practice, just casual friendly conversation. She asked about his school, his major, the usual get-to-know-you stuff. Watching them interact felt strange only because I know the secret connection between her and my workplace. Cameron has no idea about any of that history. Two people having a normal chat while I hold information that would reframe everything. The weight of knowing things you're not supposed to know.

Cameron left today and already texted that he wants to transfer here. He's probably joking but also maybe not entirely? His current school hasn't been the experience he hoped for - no real friend group, no activities he cares about, just going through the motions. Here he saw what college could be when things click. I told him to seriously consider it if he's unhappy where he is. Life's too short to stay somewhere that doesn't fit. Do you think transferring schools is generally a good idea if you're unhappy, or should people try to stick it out?

Spring break is here but I'm staying on campus to work extra shifts. The financial reality of college life means vacation is a luxury I can't quite afford. Most people have scattered to beaches or homes or travel adventures. The campus is quiet, almost eerie without the usual bustle of students. But there's something peaceful about it too. Less chaos, more space to breathe. Money over vacation was the right choice even if it's not the exciting choice.

Working extra shifts during break means it's mostly just me and Frankie holding down The Grind. Elena is around too but doing reduced hours. The slower pace has been nice actually - time to actually talk between customers instead of just rushing from order to order. Frankie and I have had more real conversations this week than in the entire previous semester. Work friendships have more room to develop when you're not constantly slammed.

Frankie and I talked more during our quiet shifts together and I learned he's actually pretty deep. Turns out he's a philosophy minor on top of whatever his main major is. We had a whole conversation about free will versus determinism while restocking cups. The guy who makes lattes and charms customers also thinks seriously about the nature of existence. People contain multitudes. I've worked alongside him for months and there's still so much I don't know about who he is outside this coffee shop.

Frankie mentioned his parents are in the restaurant industry - they own a small place back in his hometown. 'Hospitality is in the blood,' he said when explaining why he's so good at customer service. He grew up watching his parents manage a business, deal with difficult patrons, create an atmosphere that keeps people coming back. The skills feel natural to him because he absorbed them through childhood observation. Explains a lot about why he's Oscar's most trusted employee.

Got dinner with Kwame and Mira tonight, the three of us hanging out like actual friends rather than roommate-plus-RA. We went to that Thai place off campus and talked for hours about everything and nothing. Mira without her professional hat on is genuinely good company - sharp humor, interesting opinions, asks thoughtful questions. The RA dynamic that defined our early interactions is completely gone now. She's just Kwame's girlfriend who happens to also be someone I know from the dorm.

Learned more about Mira's life during dinner. She's studying public health and wants to go into healthcare administration after graduation. The RA gig makes sense now - she's drawn to systems and organization, making things run smoothly, helping people navigate bureaucracy. She has this vision of making healthcare more accessible through better management. The rule-enforcing energy I used to find intimidating is actually just her core personality: she wants things to work properly. I respect it more now that I understand it.

Students are returning from spring break and the campus is coming back to life. The quiet emptiness of the past week is being replaced by familiar chaos - voices in the hallways, lines at the dining hall, the general buzz of people existing in shared space. I didn't realize how much I'd adjusted to the silence until the noise returned. Both states have their appeal. Strangely comforting to have the normal rhythms back though. Solitude was nice but community is better.

Study group reformed after the break and Beatrice arrived with new materials she'd been preparing during her supposed vacation. The woman doesn't rest. She has fresh practice problems, updated study guides, a refined system for tackling the second half of the semester. 'Organizing for success,' she called it without irony. Dmitri and Nico looked as amused as I was by her intensity. But we all benefit from her compulsive preparation so no one complains. Beatrice keeps us alive academically.

Volleyball is back in session after the break and playoffs are approaching in April. Hana has been sending motivational videos to the group chat almost daily - clips of professional players making incredible plays, inspirational speeches from coaches, anything to keep our competitive fire burning. The woman's dedication to team morale is unmatched. Some of the videos are cheesy but they work. We're all feeling ready to make a championship run after coming so close last semester.

April approaches and with it the final stretch of junior year. The semester is winding toward its conclusion - playoffs, finals, the transition into summer. Everything feels both urgent and nostalgic at the same time. These weeks are limited and precious. Soon this particular configuration of people and experiences will shift into something new. Trying to stay present and appreciate the moment while also preparing for what comes next. Time moves whether you're ready or not.

Oscar tried an April Fools prank at work by putting salt in the sugar container. Three customers complained about their suddenly terrible drinks before he confessed and comped everyone's orders. He felt genuinely terrible about it, kept apologizing and remaking things. The prank energy did not match Oscar's personality at all. Some people are built for jokes and some people are built for sincerity. Oscar is the latter. He learned something about himself today. What are some harmless April Fools pranks that don't ruin people's food?

Zara invited me to Andre's presentation for one of his classes - something about social media and political polarization. The academic side of their relationship on display. Andre was confident and articulate, presenting research about echo chambers and algorithmic amplification. Ivy had questions, of course, because Ivy always has questions. She pushed back on some methodology choices and Andre held his ground well. Watching smart people debate smart things is entertaining even when I don't fully follow the nuances.

Orgo exam coming up and I'm determined to bounce back from the B. Ronan held extra office hours and I was there for every minute, working through practice problems and clarifying concepts that still felt shaky. His patience remains infinite. By the end of the session I felt more confident about carbonyl reactions than I have all semester. The extra preparation is necessary - I can't let one disappointing grade become a trend. This exam is redemption.

Playoff bracket came out and we have a favorable draw. First round against Harrison, who we beat easily in the regular season, then the winner moves to finals. Hana immediately started strategizing even though the Harrison game should be manageable. She never takes anything for granted, prepares for every opponent like they're championship caliber. 'Complacency kills,' she said in the group chat. Championship implications are building and the pressure feels good.

Hana created a whole strategy document for our playoff run. Not just notes, a full document with diagrams, player tendencies, set play options. FOR INTRAMURAL VOLLEYBALL. The woman's dedication borders on obsessive but results speak for themselves - we're a top team because of her leadership. She sent it to the group chat and everyone actually read it, took it seriously. When your captain puts in this much effort, you match her energy.

Mira and Kwame had their first truly public hangout at a floor event tonight. Holding hands in front of everyone, not hiding anything. She's getting transferred to a different floor next year so the official complications are being resolved. Watching them be openly affectionate after months of secrecy is sweet. The transition from forbidden RA-resident relationship to normal couple has been gradual but complete. They look happy and settled.

Celeste commented that Kwame and Mira are cute together, said 'opposites attract' while watching them interact. It's true - Kwame is chaotic creative energy and Mira is structured administrative precision. They balance each other somehow. Tobias agreed, momentarily pausing his teasing about the RA situation to acknowledge that they actually work as a couple. Even the skeptics are coming around. Some relationships just make sense once you see them in action.

Beat Harrison easily in the first playoff round. The game wasn't even close - we dominated from the first serve to the last point. Everyone played well but there was a sense of just warming up, saving our peak performance for when it matters more. Semi-finals next, one game away from another championship appearance. The team is clicking at exactly the right time. Momentum is building. We remember how close we came last year and we want it more this time.

Orgo exam done and I felt GOOD walking out. The material clicked in ways it hasn't before. Questions that would have stumped me earlier in the semester I could work through with confidence. Ronan's office hours paid off, the study group preparation paid off, everything came together. Not saying I aced it, but I know I did better than the last exam. The bounce back is possible. Can't get complacent though - still need to wait for the actual grade.

Post-exam Sunrise Diner with the study group has become ritual at this point. We claimed our usual table and processed the exam together. Dmitri ordered just coffee instead of food which felt wrong - that man should be celebrating, not conserving. He seemed in his head about something but deflected questions about it. Everyone else was in decent spirits. The relief of finishing an exam, even if you're uncertain about the results, is a specific kind of peace.

Margaret asked me today if I was 'surviving the semester' with genuine grandmotherly concern in her voice. I told her 'barely' and she laughed like I was joking even though I was completely serious. Her presence at The Grind is a constant - same time every day, same order, same warm interest in my life. She's become one of my favorite parts of working here. The regulars who treat you like a person rather than a coffee-dispensing machine make the job worthwhile.

Juno has been noticeably more confident at work lately. Today she handled a difficult customer - someone being unreasonable about their order - without spiraling into anxiety. She stayed calm, apologetic but firm, resolved the situation professionally. Growth in action. At the beginning of the year she would have fallen apart at that kind of confrontation. Now she handles it and moves on. The improvement in her self-assurance is visible. I'm proud of her development.

Oscar mentioned today that The Grind is doing better than the competition across the street. The new shop had initial buzz but customers are returning to us - the regulars never left and new people are realizing that good coffee matters more than aesthetic interiors. Oscar seemed relieved but trying not to show it. The threat to his business was real and the stress wore on him all semester. Watching that worry ease is satisfying. Quality wins in the long run.

SEMI-FINALS WON. We're going back to the championship for the second year in a row. The game was competitive but we controlled it throughout, never letting the other team feel like they could win. Championship next week. Crawford will probably be there waiting for us, another chance at the title we almost had last semester. The anticipation is already building. Everything we've worked for comes down to one more game.

Team dinner at Sunrise Diner after our semi-final victory and Hana made us go around the table sharing what we're grateful for. Corny as hell but surprisingly effective. People said genuine things about the team, about growth, about the support system we've built together. When it was my turn I talked about finding a place where I belong. The cheese was real but so was the emotion. These people mean something to me. The pancakes also help.

Sienna's gratitude share hit different. She said she's grateful the season gave her 'something good to focus on when everything else was messy.' Knowing what I know about her past year - the ex situation, the heaviness she carried, seeing Frankie at The Grind - her words carried extra weight for me. Volleyball was her anchor when personal life got turbulent. Sports as therapy. The team gave her stability when she needed it most. We all need something like that.

Orgo exam results posted and I GOT A B+! Back to where I was, back to consistent improvement territory. Professor Huang wrote 'better' on my exam which from her is practically a love letter. The bounce back happened. The extra office hours, the study group intensity, all of it paid off. One bad grade didn't become a spiral. I proved to myself I can recover from setbacks. That confidence boost might be worth more than the grade itself.

Stats grade came in too: B+. Priya got an A, obviously, because she understands statistics at a molecular level. But I'm happy with my result - passing a quantitative class with a decent grade when math has never been my strength feels like an accomplishment. The study partnership worked. I should tell Priya thanks for all her help - she probably doesn't realize how much her tutoring contributed to my success. What's a good way to thank someone who helped you academically?

Oscar gave me the day off for the championship game without me even asking. 'Go win something,' he said gruffly, like the gesture meant nothing even though scheduling coverage probably required effort. That's Oscar - does kind things while pretending he's not being kind. Having a manager who understands that life outside work matters is a genuine privilege. I promised to bring back good news. The Grind team is rooting for the volleyball team.

CHAMPIONSHIP DAY. Woke up nervous, the kind of butterflies that make it hard to eat breakfast. Everyone in the team group chat is feeling some version of the same anxiety - we've been building toward this all season, all year really. Last semester's loss still lingers as motivation. This is our chance to finish what we started. The pressure feels enormous and exhilarating simultaneously. By tonight we'll either be champions or runners-up again.

WE WON THE CHAMPIONSHIP. FIVE SETS. THE MOST INTENSE VOLLEYBALL I'VE EVER PLAYED. Down two sets to one, looked like last year all over again, then somehow found another level. Everything hurt and nothing mattered except the next point. When the final ball hit their side of the court I literally fell to my knees. We did it. CHAMPIONS. I am screaming this into the void because I have to express this somewhere. WE ARE CHAMPIONS.

Sienna's final set to Hana was PERFECT. The kind of athletic moment that burns into memory. The ball left her hands with exact precision, Hana rose to meet it, the spike was untouchable. Game over. Championship ours. They connected on that play like they'd rehearsed it a thousand times. Pure teamwork manifested as victory. Sienna's expression when she realized we'd won - relief and joy and exhaustion all at once. That image will stay with me.

The post-game celebration was chaos in the best way. Hana lifted Sienna again like last time, except this time we had the trophy to go with it. Reggie's screaming could probably be heard across campus. Coach Tran said 'adequate' which coming from him is basically hysteria - the man showed more emotion in that one word than in all his previous communications combined. We did something real. Against a team that beat us before. Champions.

Zara, Andre, and Ivy all came to the championship game and rushed the court when we won. Ivy was yelling something about 'collective triumph over adversity' because even in moments of pure joy she finds ways to be political about it. Her sign said something similar. The support from friends outside the team made the victory sweeter. These different parts of my college life - academics, social, athletic - all converging on one moment of celebration. This is what it's supposed to feel like.

Sienna gave a speech during the team celebration about how the team helped her through a hard year. She got emotional in the middle of it, voice cracking, and had to pause to collect herself. She didn't give specifics but I knew what she meant - the ex drama, the avoidance of The Grind, the weight she'd been carrying. Volleyball gave her community when she needed it most. Watching her process that gratitude publicly was moving. Sports heal in ways that aren't always visible.

Kwame and Mira showed up to the championship celebration together, fully in couple mode, no pretense of hiding anything. They looked happy and settled, congratulating me with genuine enthusiasm. The relationship that started as a secret has become completely normal to everyone. Seeing them comfortable together while I was riding the high of championship victory felt like multiple good things happening at once. This year has been full of growth and change and good endings.

Back to reality after the championship high. Finals are in two weeks and I've been so focused on volleyball that academics took a back seat. Time to shift gears completely - put the trophy mentality into studying. The season is over, the celebration has been had, now the work continues. Can't let athletic success make me complacent about academic responsibilities. Two more weeks of intense focus and then the semester is complete. I can do this.

Beatrice has the final exam study schedule ready and she sent it at 7am because she has no concept of reasonable communication hours. The document is color-coded by priority level, broken into daily goals, and includes built-in buffer time for 'unexpected challenges.' Her organizational intensity might be pathological but it's also incredibly useful. Following Beatrice's plan is easier than creating my own. She's basically the academic equivalent of a personal trainer.

Victor - I should say Dr. Victor now - stopped by The Grind to say goodbye. He's moving across the country for a postdoc position, leaving the campus and coffee shop that have been part of his life for years. He bought drinks for everyone as a farewell gesture. The regulars leaving feels significant somehow, like a chapter closing. Dr. Victor and his medieval trade route expertise will be missed. Oscar seemed genuinely sad to see him go even if he'd never admit it.

Study group is in intensive mode now. Four-hour sessions, minimal breaks, everyone focused. Beatrice brought protein bars as study fuel - practical and effective, like everything she does. We're grinding through practice problems and reviewing concepts, supporting each other through the final academic push of the semester. This group started as strangers forced together by organic chemistry and became something more. Surviving hard things together creates bonds. Can you suggest some good brain foods for studying besides protein bars?

Dmitri got into a prestigious summer research program! He's staying on campus to work in Professor Huang's lab, exactly what he hoped for when she first approached him about undergraduate research. The guy has been quiet about his applications all semester, not wanting to jinx anything. Now that it's official he seems genuinely excited, the most animated I've ever seen him. His talent is being recognized and cultivated. Watching friends succeed is almost as satisfying as succeeding yourself.

Professor Huang actually smiled when she announced Dmitri's research position to the class. A real smile, not her usual almost-smile that might be gas pain. It was a historic moment - Huang showing genuine positive emotion about a student. She clearly sees his potential and is investing in his development. The intimidating professor who terrified us all semester actually cares about her students' futures. The tough exterior hides real mentorship. Dmitri found the right person to believe in him.

Film class final is just watching a movie and writing an essay about it. Bless this class and its reasonable expectations. After a semester of intense science courses, having one class that asks me to simply engage thoughtfully with art feels like a gift. The movie is something about postwar cinema that I'm actually interested in analyzing. Sometimes education can be enjoyable. What a concept. This is the palate cleanser I needed between orgo study sessions.

Stats final is take-home, which has pros and cons. Pro: I can use notes and take my time. Con: the questions are probably harder to compensate for open book conditions. Priya says it's 'fine' but her definition of fine and my definition of fine are very different things. I'm going to need her help parsing through some of the more complex problems. Having a study partner who actually understands statistics remains my greatest academic asset.

Orgo final is in-person, three hours, no notes, pure memory and understanding. Of course it is. Professor Huang wouldn't have it any other way. The exam covers everything from the entire semester, all the mechanisms and reactions and spatial reasoning. I've been preparing for weeks but the scope feels overwhelming when I think about it too hard. Just have to trust the process - the study group work, Ronan's office hours, all of it. Three hours of my life for one grade. Here we go.

Last shift at The Grind before finals consume my life. Oscar told everyone to focus on exams for the next week, that the coffee shop will survive without us. It was his version of being supportive - acknowledging that we have lives outside this job. I appreciated the grace. Said goodbye to the regulars who'll still be there when I return, promised Margaret I'd survive the semester. The Grind will be here waiting. First I have to get through academic gauntlet.

Frankie wished me luck on my way out, genuine warmth in the farewell. 'See you on the other side,' he said, like finals are a battle we're all fighting separately. There's camaraderie among the staff that feels like more than just coworkers. We've supported each other through personal stuff, covered shifts, shared the daily grind of the Grind. These people have become friends. When I come back after exams, we'll have survived another semester together.

Juno said she's actually looking forward to summer for the first time since starting college. Her mental health journey has been progressing well - the therapy, the self-reflection, the intentional effort to build a life she enjoys here. The version of Juno who considered transferring at the beginning of the year is gone, replaced by someone who's found her footing. 'I think I'm going to be okay,' she said, and I believed her. Growth takes time but it happens. I'm glad she stayed.

ORGO FINAL DONE. Three hours of my life poured onto exam pages, every mechanism I've learned this semester extracted from my brain and committed to paper. The thing is, I actually knew things. Like, genuinely understood the material rather than just memorizing patterns. Questions that would have destroyed me in September I worked through with actual comprehension. My hand is cramping from writing but my spirit is intact. Whatever grade I get, I left everything on that exam.

Priya and I worked on the stats take-home final together, which is allowed since it's designed for collaboration. Her explanations saved me again - there were problems I would have completely botched without her walking me through the logic. We submitted around midnight, both exhausted but satisfied with our work. The partnership that started with her passing me notes in class has become genuinely important to my academic survival. Good study partners are worth their weight in gold.

SOPHOMORE YEAR COMPLETE. The last assignment submitted, the last exam finished, the academic gauntlet officially survived. I'm sitting in my dorm room feeling a strange mix of relief and nostalgia. This year pushed me harder than anything before - orgo, volleyball, work, friendships, all of it demanding pieces of my time and energy. And somehow I made it through. Not perfectly, not without struggle, but through. That counts for something.

Last Theo party of the year and maybe ever - he's graduating so this chapter of campus social life is closing. His house has been the backdrop for so many memories this year: post-game celebrations, random weekend hangouts, the Halloween party. Theo himself remains an enigma, always hosting, never seeming stressed about anything. The party had a bittersweet energy, people savoring the end of something. Finals stress was replaced by graduation anxiety for the seniors and transition feelings for everyone else.

Plot twist about Theo: he actually graduated in something practical. Accounting. This whole time I thought 'undeclared for life' was his real philosophy but apparently it was an extended bit. The guy who hosts chaotic parties and seems to float through existence without plans has been quietly completing an accounting degree. He has a job lined up at a firm and everything. Never judge a book by its party-hosting cover. Theo contains multitudes I never suspected.

Said goodbyes to the volleyball crew and it hit harder than expected. These people went from strangers at tryouts to essential parts of my life. Hana's already talking about next season, planning pickup games over summer, keeping the group chat active. Reggie gave everyone his signature bear hugs. The championship trophy is real but the relationships matter more. We built something together that goes beyond wins and losses. Already counting down to when we reunite.

Sienna's doing a summer volleyball league to keep her skills sharp and stay competitive. She seems lighter than she's been all year, like she's finally set down the heavy thing she was carrying. 'This year started hard but ended right,' she said during our goodbye. Knowing her full story now - the Frankie situation, the avoidance, the gradual healing - those words carry extra weight. She found her way through. The version of Sienna saying goodbye is healthier than the one I met in September.

Kwame and Mira were adorable all night at the end-of-year party, fully comfortable as a public couple now. She's transferring floors next year to eliminate any remaining conflict of interest, and they're already talking about getting an off-campus apartment together eventually. The relationship that started as a secret has become stable and official. Watching them together, you'd never guess it began with noise complaints and paranoid speaker-hiding. Love finds weird paths sometimes.

Packing to leave campus and once again I'm confronted by the sheer volume of stuff I've accumulated. Where did all of this come from?
