Books I forgot I bought, clothes that migrated from home, random objects with no clear origin story. The process of condensing a year of life into transportable boxes is both tedious and reflective. Each item is a small memory. The volleyball I kept from a particularly good game. The coffee mug Frankie gave me. Artifacts of a year well lived.

Kwame and I confirmed we're rooming together again next year, which wasn't really in question but felt good to make official. Some roommate pairings are luck - you get assigned someone random and hope for the best. We got lucky and then chose to continue. He's the right amount of social without being intrusive, respectful of space and sleep, genuinely good company. The Tuesday night pastry detective sessions alone are worth the commitment. Some things just work.

Grades are posting and the anxiety of waiting is almost worse than the exams themselves. Film class: A. Expected but still satisfying - engaging with cinema thoughtfully is apparently something I can do. Stats: B+, which matches my expectations and Priya's tutoring investment. Still waiting on orgo, the one that matters most. Refreshing the grade portal obsessively even though I know it won't make the number appear faster. The suspense is genuinely difficult.

ORGO 2: A-!! I GOT AN A-!! Professor Huang gave me an A-!! I had to check three times to make sure I was reading it correctly. This is the class that terrified me, the professor who intimidated me, the material that made me question my academic capabilities. And I got an A-. Not just passing, not just decent, but genuinely good. The journey from scared freshman to A- student feels like a complete transformation. I might actually be good at this.

Professor Huang wrote 'significant improvement, well done' on my final grade report. FROM HUANG. The woman who communicates through intimidating silences and almost-smiles gave me written positive feedback. I'm genuinely considering framing this. The validation from someone whose standards are impossibly high means more than any easy A ever could. She saw me struggle and watched me grow and acknowledged the growth. This might be the proudest academic moment of my life so far.

Called mom to share the orgo grade and ended up crying happy tears on the phone. She was so proud, kept saying she knew I could do it even when I didn't believe it myself. Dad got on the line and said 'good job kid' in his understated way that somehow conveys deep emotion. Grandma Lucia, who was apparently visiting, grabbed the phone to tell me she always knew I was a scientist in the making. The whole family celebration over a chemistry grade. It matters to them because it matters to me.

Stopped by The Grind to say goodbye before leaving campus. Oscar was behind the counter looking exactly the same as always - slightly grumpy exterior hiding genuine care for his staff. He told me I have a job whenever I want it, that I'm 'one of the good ones.' For Oscar, that's practically a declaration of love. The coffee shop that gave me income and community and accidental drama will be here waiting when I return. It's become part of my college identity.

Frankie and Juno were both working when I stopped by for my goodbye visit. We had a group hug that probably looked unprofessional to any customers watching. 'Don't be a stranger,' Frankie said, and I promised I wouldn't. These coworkers became friends over shared shifts and mutual support through personal struggles. Juno looks so different from the overwhelmed girl who had breakdowns over customer complaints. Frankie seems fully healed from the ex situation. We all grew this year.

Oscar stayed behind the counter during the farewell moment, not joining the group hug because that's not his style. But he nodded at me in a way that communicated everything words would have said. Respect, appreciation, the acknowledgment of a job well done. Oscar's nods are a language unto themselves - you have to learn to read the subtle variations. This particular nod meant 'you're valued here and that won't change.' I nodded back. We understood each other.

The pigeon appeared at my window one last time as I was doing final packing. Same ledge, same intense stare, like it knew this was goodbye for the summer. We've had this strange relationship all year - it watching me, me wondering what it wants, neither of us ever resolving the dynamic. I waved at it, which I'm aware is absurd. See you in September, weird bird. Try not to miss me too much. The surveillance will resume when I return.

Home. Two semesters of growth compressed into a bus ride back to where I started. My childhood room feels both familiar and foreign now - the same posters on the wall, the same furniture arrangement, but I'm different. A volleyball championship. A B+ to A- improvement in orgo. Real friendships that will last beyond the semester. I left home a kid trying to figure out college and came back someone who actually figured some of it out. That's not nothing.

Got a summer job at the local coffee shop near home because apparently making drinks is now my transferable skill. It's not The Grind - different vibe, different regulars, different management style - but the muscle memory of espresso machines and customer service translates. The money will help with next year's expenses. Working while everyone else seems to be on vacation isn't glamorous but it's practical. Building toward something requires sacrifices in the present.

Cameron and I are planning a road trip for July and the route planning has become its own form of entertainment. We're mapping out every weird roadside attraction, every diner with suspicious but intriguing reviews, every 'world's largest' something. The trip isn't just about the destination, it's about the intentionally ridiculous journey. After a semester of structure and stress, some unstructured adventure with my oldest friend sounds perfect. What are some must-see quirky roadside attractions in the midwest?

The road trip was everything we hoped for and more. Found the 'world's biggest ball of yarn' and took appropriately ironic photos pretending to be impressed. Ate at diners where the food was questionable but the experience was authentic. Got lost multiple times and discovered places we never would have found on purpose. Cameron and I have been friends since middle school but this trip added new layers to our history. Adventures with the right person become core memories.

Volleyball meetup with Hana, Reggie, and some others from the team who are in the area. We found a sand court and played for hours, the summer heat making everything more intense. My skills are still there, the chemistry with teammates still clicks. Hana was in full competitive mode even for a casual game because she doesn't know how to not compete. It felt like a preview of next season - the hunger is still there, maybe stronger after winning the championship.

Grandma Lucia invited me over for what she called the 'final recipe lesson' - teaching me her famous sauce that she's never shared with anyone outside the family. It felt ceremonial, like being inducted into a secret society. She walked me through every step, every timing detail, every technique she's refined over decades. The sauce that's been at every family gathering my whole life, now I know how to make it. Full circle from eating it as a kid to carrying the tradition forward.

Grandma Lucia is my mom's mom, the Italian grandmother stereotype made flesh but also so much more than that. She survived things, built a life, raised children who raised me. The recipe lesson wasn't just about sauce - it was about connection, about passing something down, about her trusting me to carry a piece of her forward. 'You cook this for your family someday,' she said. The weight of that expectation felt like a gift rather than a burden. What's the best way to preserve family recipes for future generations?

Packing for the return to campus and facing the annual struggle of deciding what to bring. Every year I think I've figured it out and every year I'm wrong. Too many clothes, not enough of the right supplies, forgotten essentials that require emergency purchases. The transition from home life to dorm life requires a mental shift that the packing process initiates. Same struggle, different year. At least this time I know what I actually need versus what I think I need.

BACK ON CAMPUS. Junior year begins. Linford Hall looks exactly the same as when I left - same brick exterior, same slightly musty hallway smell, same fluorescent lighting that makes everyone look vaguely ill. But there's comfort in the familiarity now. This place has become a second home. Kwame's car was already in the lot when I arrived, which means he's here and our room is probably already half-claimed by his photography equipment.

THE PIGEON IS BACK. Not just back - it brought reinforcements. There are now THREE pigeons on my window ledge, all staring into my room with synchronized intensity. It's not surveillance anymore, it's a tribunal. They've convened to judge my summer activities and find me wanting. Kwame thinks I'm losing it but he doesn't have to wake up to a panel of avian observers every morning. The pigeon situation has escalated and I'm not sure what the endgame is.

First shift back at The Grind and it felt like stepping into a comfortable routine. Everything's the same but also slightly different - new seasonal menu items, a rearranged pastry display, but the essential Grind-ness remains. Oscar greeted me with his standard gruffness that now reads as affection. Frankie's been developing new latte art skills over the summer and showed off some impressive designs. The coffee shop family reunited for another year of caffeine and customer service.

Margaret walked into The Grind and immediately said 'there's my favorite' when she saw me behind the counter. The warmth in her voice made my whole day better. She's been coming here for years, watched staff come and go, and apparently I've made the list of people she genuinely likes. Her order is the same as always, her tip generous as always, her grandmotherly concern about my wellbeing as present as always. Some regulars become like family. Margaret is one of those.

Volleyball tryouts for new members to replace the people who graduated. Watching freshmen try out reminds me of being in their position not that long ago - nervous, eager to impress, hoping to find a place on the team. We lost some key players to graduation so fresh talent is necessary. Hana is taking the evaluation process very seriously, clipboard and notes and analytical assessment of every candidate. The championship defense requires the right personnel additions.

One of the tryout standouts is a freshman named Nadia who has serious skills. Her footwork is clean, her court awareness is advanced for her age, and she has this competitive fire that reminds me of Sienna. Hana noticed her immediately and I could see the wheels turning - where to slot her into our system, how to develop her potential. Having someone with raw talent join the team could be exactly what we need to stay at championship level.

Another promising new player is Marcus, who's absurdly tall like Reggie but less naturally coordinated. The height is there, the instincts need development. With coaching and practice time he could become a real asset as a blocker. Hana's already talking about training programs to maximize his potential. The combination of Nadia's polished skills and Marcus's raw physical tools gives the team interesting options for the season ahead.

Wait, there's already a Marcus somewhere in my life. I spent a minute confused before realizing the Marcus I was thinking of is someone Cameron mentioned from his school, not anyone I actually know directly. Different guy entirely. The overlap of common names across different social circles gets confusing sometimes. New volleyball Marcus has no connection to Cameron's Marcus. My brain just pattern-matched incorrectly. Names are hard when you know too many people.

Our new RA this year is a guy named Derek. He seems fine - professional, by-the-book, clearly takes the role seriously. But there's a stiffness to him that Mira never had, even at her most official. He did the floor meeting with practiced efficiency, covered all the rules and policies, answered questions without personality. Functional but not warm. I miss Mira's underlying humanity even when she was enforcing quiet hours. Derek is an upgrade in competence maybe, but a downgrade in connection.

The Grind hired someone new over the summer while I was gone - a grad student named Grace who radiates efficiency. She's doing her PhD in something science-related and treats the coffee shop job with the same systematic precision she probably applies to her research. Within her first week she reorganized the storage area and improved the inventory tracking system. Oscar seems impressed and slightly intimidated by her competence. Having another adult on staff changes the dynamic.

Watching Frankie train Grace is strange because it positions him as the senior, experienced staff member. Which he is, but seeing him in that mentorship role highlights how long he's been at The Grind and how much institutional knowledge he carries. He knows things about the espresso machine's quirks, about specific customers' preferences, about Oscar's management patterns. Grace is learning from him the same way I once did. The cycle of coffee shop wisdom continues.

Study group reformed for junior year but the composition has shifted. Beatrice and Dmitri are still core members, our orgo veterans. Nico's schedule doesn't align with ours anymore so he's out, which is sad but these things happen. We need to recruit new people to fill the gaps. The group that carried me through sophomore year chemistry is evolving. Some friendships transcend the circumstances that created them, others are more situational. Both types have value.

We recruited Sanjay to the study group - he's a friend of Dmitri's from the research lab, quiet but clearly intelligent. He has that same understated competence that makes Dmitri so valuable, answering questions with precision and patience. Beatrice interrogated him about his study habits before approving his addition, which felt excessive but is very on-brand for her. He passed whatever internal evaluation she was running. The study group has a new member.

Beatrice's interrogation of Sanjay was thorough - she asked about his note-taking methods, his review schedule, whether he's the type to pull weight in group settings or coast on others' work. Sanjay answered everything calmly, apparently unbothered by the vetting process. He passed her standards, which are not low. 'He'll do,' Beatrice declared, which is her version of a warm welcome. The study group's quality control remains strict. Standards matter when grades are on the line.

New orgo professor this year - Dr. Patel instead of Professor Huang. He assigned a group project in the first week, which is a different vibe entirely. Got paired with a girl named Leah who seems competent and some guy whose name I didn't catch. Group projects are always a gamble - you either get people who pull their weight or people who disappear until the deadline. Hoping for the former but preparing mentally for the latter.

The guy in my orgo group project is also named Frankie. Two Frankies in my life now, which is going to require mental disambiguation. Work Frankie is the barista, Sienna's ex, philosophy minor, someone I've known for over a year. Class Frankie is a random guy I just met who wears a lot of flannel. Completely different people sharing a name. I'm going to need a system to keep them straight in my head and in conversation.

Mental note for disambiguation: Work Frankie is the one at The Grind, has the complicated ex history with Sienna, does philosophy, has been around since I started college. Class Frankie is the orgo project guy, wears flannel constantly, I know nothing else about him yet. Work Frankie and Class Frankie. Barista Frankie and Flannel Frankie. I'll figure out a naming convention that sticks. The important thing is not mixing them up in conversation and creating confusion.

First game of the volleyball season against Pemberton and we won easily. The new players are integrating well, Nadia especially showing flashes of real talent. The team chemistry from last year's championship run hasn't dissipated - if anything, the shared history makes us stronger. Defending the title starts with games like this, building momentum through consistent wins. One down, a season to go.

Nadia's first game was more than just solid - she looked like she belonged out there with players who've been together for years. Her instincts are sharp, her nerves didn't show, she took coaching feedback and applied it immediately. Hana was practically glowing with satisfaction at her scouting choice paying off. Finding someone who can step into a championship team and contribute right away is rare. Nadia might be special.

Sienna seems fully herself now, the best version I've seen since we met. No weird tension, no cloud of unprocessed emotion, just present and engaged and enjoying volleyball. The stuff that weighed on her last year - the Frankie situation, the avoidance, the healing process - all of it seems resolved. She's leading younger players, contributing to team morale, playing at her highest level. Growth completed. It's good to see someone come out the other side of hard things stronger.

After practice, Sienna mentioned offhandedly that she's 'finally moved past last year's mess.' She said it casually, like it was old news hardly worth discussing, which is exactly how you talk about things you've actually processed versus things you're still struggling with. The mess had a name - Frankie - and a location - The Grind - but she doesn't need to know I figured that out. Her healing is her business. I'm just glad she's through it.

Grace is starting to open up a bit at work, sharing details about her life beyond the efficient coffee-making exterior. She's doing her PhD in environmental science, specifically studying microplastics in freshwater ecosystems. When she talks about her research, her usually businesslike demeanor softens into genuine enthusiasm. Finding out a coworker has a whole other intellectual life going on always makes them more interesting. Grace contains depths beneath the organizational competence.

Oscar seems to appreciate having another adult on staff in Grace. They talk about things like tax implications and retirement planning and lease negotiations - adult stuff that the rest of us college students don't engage with yet. It's a different dynamic than Oscar has with me or Frankie or Juno or Elena. Grace is a peer in ways we can't be yet. Maybe it's good for Oscar to have someone who understands the non-student perspective on life and work.

Tobias and Celeste survived long distance over the summer and came back apparently stronger than before. Whatever issues caused those arguments I overheard through the walls seem to be resolved. They're openly affectionate in the hallway, finishing each other's sentences, that nauseating couple energy that's only acceptable when you genuinely like both people. Good for them for making it work. Long distance tests relationships and many don't survive. Theirs did.

Derek the new RA tried to organize a floor bonding event tonight - some kind of icebreaker game situation in the common room. The turnout was sparse, maybe six people out of twenty-plus floor residents. He seemed disappointed but tried to make the best of it with whoever showed up. I felt bad for him, honestly. Mira had an easier time building floor community, but maybe that's just because she'd been here longer. Derek deserves a chance. How do you think RAs can build community when residents seem uninterested?

One of the freshmen who actually came to Derek's event is a guy named Jason who turns out to be pretty cool. He's a film major, which immediately gave us common ground from my cinema gen ed. We talked about Hitchcock for twenty minutes - he has strong opinions about Vertigo and I respect that even though I disagree with some of his takes. Found an unexpected connection at an awkward floor event. Sometimes showing up to things pays off socially.

Priya texted asking if I wanted to study together for stats again this semester. Obviously yes - her help last year was instrumental in me passing. We're taking the sequel course together and the material is reportedly harder, so having a study partner who actually understands statistics is even more essential. The partnership that started with her passing me notes has become a reliable academic collaboration. Some connections are worth maintaining deliberately.

The guy with the complicated coffee order has evolved his specifications even further. The oat milk latte with specific pump counts and temperature requirements now also has a particular ice situation - not too much, not too little, arranged in a certain way that I didn't know was possible to specify. Every time I think I've mastered his order he adds another layer. At this point I respect the commitment to customization even as I struggle to execute it. What even qualifies as a reasonable coffee customization versus unreasonable?

Group project meeting with Leah and Class Frankie. Leah is clearly going to do most of the work - she arrived with an outline, a timeline, and assigned sections for each of us. Class Frankie mostly nodded along without contributing much. I'm somewhere in the middle, willing to pull my weight but grateful someone else is taking charge. The group project dynamic is playing out exactly as predicted: one leader, one follower, one person trying to stay useful.

Dmitri finally beat Howard, the chess guy in the quad! Their rivalry has been building all year and today Dmitri emerged victorious. I wasn't there to witness it but apparently it was a close match that drew a small crowd of spectators. Howard, who has beaten everyone for years, looked genuinely surprised at the outcome. They're now officially 'rivals' according to Dmitri, which means regular rematches are scheduled. The student finally surpassed the master, at least temporarily.

Learned more about Howard he's a retired physics professor who's been playing in that quad spot for over a decade. He's legendary among people who pay attention to such things. The fact that Dmitri beat him is actually a significant achievement in campus chess lore, not that most students care about campus chess lore. But Howard took the loss gracefully and complimented Dmitri's endgame. Respect between competitors. Good sportsmanship crosses all games.

Professor Whiskers, the friendly cat who lives near the science building, has been officially adopted by the library staff. They set up a little bed for it near the entrance and it greets students as they come in. The cat that used to just hang around is now an institutional mascot with a designated sleeping spot. The library feels warmer with Professor Whiskers as an official resident. Sometimes bureaucracy produces good outcomes, like legitimizing a campus cat.

Mira and Kwame are already talking about getting an apartment together next year when they're both seniors. The relationship has progressed from secret RA-resident thing to fully integrated life planning. They're looking at places off campus, discussing budgets, imagining a shared space that's theirs. Watching them plan a future together is sweet even as it makes me realize Kwame won't be my roommate forever. Change is constant. Good things evolve into different good things.

Kwame asked if I'd be okay without a roommate if he moves in with Mira next year. The question made the future suddenly concrete - no more pastry detective nights, no more shared room, the end of our particular domestic arrangement. I told him I'd be fine, which is true, but there's sadness in it too. He's my first real college roommate and we got lucky with each other. That chapter will close eventually. Trying to appreciate it while it lasts.

Work Frankie mentioned he might move to part-time next semester because he's applying to grad schools. The applications are consuming his time and mental energy, and working full shifts while trying to craft personal statements is unsustainable. It makes sense but the news landed with unexpected weight. Frankie has been at The Grind since before I started, one of the constants of the place. Things are going to change there too.

Oscar looked concerned when Frankie mentioned potentially reducing his hours. Frankie is his most trusted employee, the one who handles crises and knows all the institutional knowledge. Losing him, even partially, represents a real operational challenge. Oscar didn't say much but you could see the wheels turning, planning for a future where Frankie isn't as available. The Grind is a small business that runs on specific people. Losing key people matters.

Oscar mentioned during a quiet moment that Frankie has been at The Grind 'since the beginning.' I asked what that meant and apparently Frankie started working there his freshman year - he's been around almost as long as the shop has existed in its current form. Oscar was there from actual day one, but Frankie is the most senior employee by a wide margin. The place is what it is partly because Frankie helped shape it. That history matters.

Game against Bradley was closer than expected but we pulled through. Marcus, the tall new player, is getting better with each game - still raw but improving visibly. Nadia had another strong performance, cementing her place in the rotation. The team is developing depth we didn't have last year. Championship defense requires everyone contributing, not just the stars. The supporting cast is stepping up.

Sienna's leadership is emerging in ways that go beyond her on-court excellence. She's mentoring younger players like Nadia, giving tips and encouragement, sharing knowledge she's accumulated over years of competitive volleyball. Watching her step into that role is gratifying - she has so much to offer and now she's positioned to pass it on. The team benefits from her experience. The players coming up will be better because she taught them.

Someone at the post-game hangout asked Sienna if she was single, probably attempting to flirt. She laughed and said 'happily so' with a lightness that felt genuine rather than defensive. She's not looking for anyone right now, not avoiding relationships out of hurt but choosing independence out of preference. That's healthy. That's someone who's done the work and emerged confident in her own company. The contrast to where she was at the beginning of last year is stark.

The dining hall introduced 'international cuisine Wednesdays' with rotating themes from different countries. Today was supposedly Mexican food. The execution was questionable at best - the tacos were fine but calling them authentic would be generous. Still, the concept is good even if the implementation needs work. Exposing students to different food cultures, even imperfect versions, has value. Maybe the Italian week will be better. Or maybe I'm just being optimistic.

First exam of the semester is approaching and the familiar stress patterns are activating. Dr. Patel's class is supposedly easier than Huang's was, but easier is relative when the subject matter is still organic chemistry. The study group has been meeting regularly, working through practice problems, building toward exam readiness. At least this time I know the process works. The anxiety is familiar now, a known quantity I can manage rather than an overwhelming unknown.

The new coffee shop in the student center is struggling. Every time I walk by, it's nearly empty while The Grind across campus has consistent customers. The initial novelty has worn off and people have realized that aesthetic alone doesn't make good coffee. Oscar doesn't gloat about it but you can tell he's relieved. The competitive threat that stressed him out is fading. Quality and community beat trendy design in the long run.

Victor - Dr. Victor now - apparently emailed Oscar from his postdoc position to check in on how things are going at The Grind. Even though he's across the country doing important research on medieval trade routes, he still thinks about the coffee shop that was part of his routine for years. Oscar mentioned it offhandedly but seemed touched by the continued connection. The Grind creates relationships that persist beyond geography. That's special for a coffee shop.

First exam went fine. Not spectacular, not disastrous, just fine. Dr. Patel is a fair test-writer - the questions were challenging but they were the kind of challenging we prepared for. No trick questions designed to destroy confidence, just straightforward assessment of whether we understood the material. Walking out I felt neutral, which is honestly better than the shell-shocked feeling after Huang exams. Different professor energy makes a difference.

Kwame's photography exhibit is coming up and he's been working on it obsessively for months. The 'people in their natural environments' concept has expanded into something more ambitious - a full show of portraits that tell stories about the subjects' lives through visual composition. He's nervous in a way I don't usually see from him. This matters to him, this is his art being judged publicly. I'm planning to be there for support.

Halloween is approaching and people are starting to discuss costumes. I've decided to go as a chemistry student, which sounds lazy but I'm going to commit to the bit - actual lab coat, safety goggles, fake test tubes with colored liquid, maybe some controlled chaos in my appearance. The joke is that I basically am a chemistry student, but making it a costume adds intentionality. Sometimes the meta-costume is funnier than elaborate creativity.

Kwame and Mira are going as a couples costume that's too clever for its own good. He's going as 'a developing photograph' with a black outfit that has an image slowly appearing on it. She's going as 'overexposed film' with a washed-out light aesthetic. Photography puns as costume concept. They're THAT couple now, the ones who coordinate costumes around their shared interests. It's cute in a way that would be annoying if I didn't love them both.

The squirrels on the quad have entered aggressive autumn hoarding mode. I watched one steal an entire sandwich from someone's bag with military precision - approach, assess, grab, flee. The fearlessness of campus wildlife continues to escalate. They've moved beyond accepting offered food to actively taking whatever they want. Somewhere a squirrel is eating someone's lunch and feeling no remorse. Natural selection favors the bold I guess.

Study group intensive resumed as exams approach. Beatrice brought a whiteboard to our session, which raises questions about where she obtained it and how she transported it. She didn't explain and nobody asked. The whiteboard has become part of our setup now, used for working through complex problems together. Beatrice's inexplicable resource acquisition is just part of how she operates. Accept the whiteboard, don't question the whiteboard.

Nico texted the old study group chat saying he misses us, which was unexpectedly sweet. His schedule this semester doesn't overlap with ours but he still thinks about the people who helped him survive orgo. We're planning a reunion dinner to maintain the connection even though we can't study together anymore. Some friendships outlast the circumstances that created them. Nico and his cookies will always have a place in the group history.

Work Frankie's grad school applications are officially submitted and now he's in the waiting period that's apparently worse than the preparation period. He keeps checking his email obsessively even though decisions won't come for months. The anxiety of putting yourself out there and waiting for judgment is universal whether it's exams or job applications or graduate school. I told him he's going to get in somewhere great. He's one of the most capable people I know.

Oscar told Frankie directly that he has a job at The Grind no matter what happens with grad school applications. The safety net was offered without Frankie having to ask for it. Frankie got genuinely emotional at the reassurance, which is rare for him. Oscar's gruff exterior hides real care for his employees. Knowing you have somewhere to land if plans fall through matters more than people admit. The Grind is home base for a lot of us.

Game against Milton was closer than expected - they've improved since last year and pushed us hard. We won but it required full effort, no coasting on superior talent. Sienna had an off day but Nadia stepped up in a big way, proving that our depth is real. You can't win championships with only your starters playing well. The whole roster needs to contribute and tonight the backup players earned their spots.

Exam week was brutal this round - three exams in four days with barely time to breathe between them. The schedule felt designed to maximize stress. Survived through caffeine, study groups, and the acceptance that perfect preparation wasn't possible. Sometimes you just have to do your best with limited time and trust that it's enough. The gauntlet is over. Now the waiting for results begins, which is its own form of torture.

EXAMS DONE. The relief of finishing the last one is physical - shoulders dropping, jaw unclenching, the baseline anxiety that's been constant for weeks finally quieting. Whatever grades come, the work is complete. Nothing more I can do now except wait. Freedom tastes like the absence of pending obligations. A few days of mental rest before the next cycle of assignments and stress begins. Enjoying the window while it lasts.

Original study group reunion at Sunrise Diner to celebrate surviving midterms. Beatrice, Dmitri, Nico, and me - the core four who made it through Huang's orgo together. We claimed a booth and caught up on everything - Nico's new schedule, Dmitri's research, Beatrice's med school preparations. The conversation flowed easily despite weeks of not seeing each other. Some groups just work, the chemistry lasting beyond the chemistry class that brought us together.

Nico brought his famous cookies to the reunion because of course he did. The brown butter secret is out but somehow they still taste just as good when you know how they're made. We passed them around the table while catching up, the familiar flavor connecting us to all those late-night study sessions. Food as memory, taste as time travel. These cookies will always remind me of surviving organic chemistry with this specific group of people.

We talked for three hours without running out of things to say. Life updates flowed into philosophical tangents flowed into reminiscing about shared struggles. Nico's doing better academically this semester. Dmitri's research is producing interesting results. Beatrice has a spreadsheet tracking her med school application timeline because she can't not have a spreadsheet for everything. These people started as strangers in a study group and became real friends. Some friendships just work.

HALLOWEEN. My chemistry student costume was a hit - the lab coat and safety goggles and fake test tubes got laughs from people who recognized the meta-humor of dressing as what I actually am. The party at Theo's replacement venue (since Theo graduated) was packed with elaborate costumes. Halloween brings out creativity in people who normally dress in jeans and hoodies. One night of collective transformation.

Kwame and Mira's matching costume absolutely won best couple at the party. The 'developing photograph' and 'overexposed film' concept was clever enough that people kept asking them to explain it, which they did with coordinated enthusiasm. They're so in sync now it's almost nauseating but in the good way. The couple that puns together stays together apparently. Their relationship has become a stable, healthy presence in their respective friend groups.

Midterm grades trickling in and the news is good. Stats: B+, consistent with expectations and Priya's tutoring investment. Patel's orgo class: A-, which actually exceeds what I thought I'd get. The study systems that worked sophomore year are still working. Not coasting on past success but not reinventing the wheel either. Find what works and keep doing it. Academic strategy is just applied pattern recognition.

Margaret brought me a scarf she knitted herself because apparently I 'looked cold last week.' Hand-knitted by an elderly regular customer because she was worried about my warmth. The gesture hit me unexpectedly hard - such a pure expression of care from someone who doesn't have to care at all. I'm just the person who makes her coffee. But she notices things about my wellbeing and responds with handmade gifts. Margaret is the grandmother of The Grind and I'm lucky to know her.

FRANKIE GOT INTO HIS TOP GRAD SCHOOL CHOICE! He found out during his shift and literally jumped in the air behind the counter, nearly knocking over a stack of cups. The months of applications and anxiety and waiting, all resolved in one acceptance email. He's going to a great program for his field. The Grind celebrated with him, Oscar giving a rare smile, everyone hugging and congratulating. Doors opening for deserving people is the best kind of news.

Oscar hugged Frankie when the grad school news broke, which is shocking behavior from a man who usually communicates through grunts and nods. Physical affection is not Oscar's language but the moment apparently warranted an exception. Frankie has been more than an employee to Oscar - he's been a partner in building what The Grind has become. Watching them share that moment was genuinely touching. Some professional relationships transcend professionalism.

The reality is setting in that Frankie is leaving. He's staying through spring semester but then he's gone, off to grad school in another state. The Grind without Frankie is hard to imagine - he's been a constant since before I started. Oscar's already thinking about succession, who can step into the institutional knowledge role. Things change, people move on, that's how life works. But I'm going to miss him when he goes.

Volleyball team is 8-0, undefeated heading into the final stretch of the regular season. The championship implications are building. We've been here before, we know what's at stake, we know what it takes to win it all. Last year we proved we could do it. This year we need to prove it wasn't a fluke. The pressure feels different when you're defending rather than chasing. Everyone expects us to win now. We expect it too.

Sienna and I ran extra drills after practice, her competitive fire burning brighter as playoffs approach. She's playing the best volleyball of her college career right now, all the talent finally unencumbered by personal drama. Watching her excel is satisfying because I know what she went through to get here. The struggle was real but she came out stronger. Athletics as a vehicle for personal growth, not just competition.

Kwame's inviting Mira to Thanksgiving with his family, which is a significant relationship milestone. Meeting the parents, holiday integration, the whole serious commitment package. They've been together for less than a year but the trajectory is clearly heading somewhere permanent. I'm happy for him even as it makes me aware of my own single status. Not jealous exactly, just conscious of different life paths diverging. What's appropriate to bring as a gift when meeting your partner's parents for the first time?

Andre's birthday party was very on-brand - philosophical discussions, quality wine, someone inevitably starting a debate about ethics or epistemology or something equally heady. At one point three people were arguing about whether birthday cake is morally neutral while I just ate my slice in silence. Andre seemed to enjoy the intellectual chaos, laughing at the absurdity of his friends being unable to not philosophize even during a celebration. These are my people now. Overthinkers celebrating together.

Group project with Leah and Class Frankie is finally done. We turned it in after weeks of meetings and revisions and the usual group project frustrations. Leah did the heavy lifting as expected. Class Frankie eventually contributed a full section toward the end, surprising everyone with actual work after weeks of nodding along. I held up my part of the middle ground. The final product is solid. Group projects always teach you more about people management than course content.

Plot twist: Class Frankie actually came through at the end with solid contributions. His section of the project was well-researched and clearly written, contradicting my expectations of him being a passenger. Maybe he's just a slow starter who does better under deadline pressure. Or maybe he was dealing with stuff earlier in the semester that's now resolved. Either way, my judgment was premature. People surprise you when you give them chances to.

Thanksgiving at home was the usual beautiful chaos. Too much food, too many relatives, conversations overlapping in multiple rooms. Grandma Lucia held court in the kitchen, directing operations like a general. Mom and her sisters navigated complex family dynamics with practiced ease. Dad and the uncles watched football and avoided the kitchen politics. I sat at the kids' table even though I'm twenty because that's just how the seating works. Perfect in its imperfection.

Cameron texted that he's definitely coming to the championship game if we make it. He wants to see in person what he glimpsed during his campus visit - the team energy, the competition, the community I've built here. Having my oldest friend witness my college athletic life would mean a lot. First we have to make the championship, which isn't guaranteed. But the pressure of wanting to win so Cameron can see it adds motivation. Stakes on top of stakes.

Back on campus after Thanksgiving and the December grind officially begins. The dorm felt weirdly quiet when I got back, like everyone's already in study mode. Kwame was at his desk with three textbooks open and didn't even look up when I walked in. The energy has shifted from 'college fun' to 'survival mode' real quick. I unpacked my stuff, including the leftover turkey sandwiches mom insisted I bring, and just sat there for a minute realizing how fast this semester went.

The schedule for the next two weeks is absolutely brutal. Semi-finals for volleyball are next weekend, which means intense practices on top of everything else, and then finals week for actual school hits right after. I made a calendar trying to map it all out and it looks like a war zone. Hana's already sending motivational texts to the group chat every six hours. Beatrice sent her 'Finals Preparation Protocol' document. I'm surrounded by overachievers and I'm just trying to survive.

Just realized we're 9-0 going into playoffs. Completely undefeated this season. Back to back championship runs and now we're gunning for the three-peat. When I say it out loud it sounds insane. This time last year I was just happy to make the team and now we're legitimate contenders. Hana printed out our record and taped it to her door like a battle flag. Coach Tran nodded approvingly, which is basically a standing ovation from him.

SEMI-FINALS WON. It wasn't pretty, went to four sets, but we pulled through when it mattered. Sienna was absolutely locked in during the final set, her focus was almost scary. Finals are against Crawford again because of course they are. It's like the universe wants this rivalry to be a thing. Their captain gave us this look across the net after the game that said 'see you soon.' The tension is real and honestly I live for it.

Study group intensive session tonight. Beatrice brought protein bars, color-coded flashcards, and what I can only describe as 'aggressive optimism.' Dmitri walked us through the hardest organic chemistry concepts like he was explaining how to make toast. Nico showed up late but actually contributed for once. We're in the final push before finals and everyone's running on caffeine and determination. The library basement has become our war room.

ORGO FINAL DONE. Three hours of my life I'll never get back but honestly? I knew things. Like actually knew them. When I saw the mechanism questions I didn't panic, I just worked through them step by step like Dmitri taught us. Walked out feeling lighter than I have in weeks. Beatrice was already outside comparing answers with random people. I told her to stop because what's done is done. She did not stop.

Stats take-home submitted. Film essay submitted. Everything is submitted. I am officially done with fall semester academics and the relief is hitting me in waves. My brain feels like it's been wrung out like a sponge. Kwame found me lying on the floor staring at the ceiling and just stepped over me without comment. He gets it. Now I just need to channel whatever energy I have left into the championship game tomorrow.

CHAMPIONSHIP DAY AND WE WON AGAIN. BACK TO BACK CHAMPIONS. I can barely type this because my hands are shaking. It went to five sets against Crawford and every single point in that final set felt like life or death. When Hana's spike hit the floor for the winning point the gym erupted. I think I screamed so loud I lost my voice. We did it. We actually did it again.

Sienna was named MVP and she absolutely deserved it. Her performance in the clutch moments was unreal, like she became a different person when the pressure was highest. Hana sobbed into her jersey for a solid five minutes. I sobbed. Reggie sobbed, which was jarring because he's usually so stoic. Everyone sobbed. The trophy presentation was a blur of tears and hugs and camera flashes. This team is something special.

Cameron made it to the championship! He drove four hours to watch us play and was screaming louder than anyone in the stands. When we won he rushed the court even though he definitely wasn't supposed to and just bear-hugged me while yelling 'THAT'S MY BEST FRIEND' to anyone who would listen. Having him there made the whole thing feel more real somehow, like my two worlds finally collided in the best way.

Grades are in and I got another A- in orgo. Consistency is my brand apparently. But honestly, an A- from the orgo sequence feels like a victory. Huang would be proud, I think. Patel wrote 'excellent work' on my final which is high praise from him. The journey from barely surviving freshman chemistry to this moment feels surreal. I texted the study group and we're all celebrating virtually since everyone's scattered for break.

Found out Oscar has been at The Grind since day one. Like literally since it opened. He was the first employee, started as a barista himself, worked his way up to manager. He told me this while we were closing up and it completely reframed how I see him. All those stressed looks and particular ways of doing things make sense now. This place is basically his baby. No wonder he takes everything so personally.

Said goodbye to Frankie before winter break. His last day is in the spring but this felt like the real farewell since we won't overlap much next semester with his reduced hours. He gave me this weird bro-hug and said 'hold it down for me.' Oscar pretended not to watch but I saw him glance over. Things change, people move on. It's the natural order but it still feels weird knowing the Grind won't be the same.

Home for winter break and it feels different this time. Junior year is complete, which means I'm officially more than halfway done with college. Two years down, less than two to go. Mom made my favorite dinner and dad actually asked about my classes instead of just sports. Grandma Lucia is coming tomorrow with enough food to feed a small army. The house smells like pine and cinnamon and childhood. I needed this.

Grandma Lucia called for her regular check-in and spent the first ten minutes asking if I've found 'someone nice' yet. I told her I'm focusing on school and she made this skeptical humming sound that only grandmothers can make. Then she pivoted to asking about my grades and when I told her about the A- she said 'that's my smart grandbaby' and I could hear her getting emotional. She's sending cookies next week.

Sitting here on Christmas night doing the annual reflection thing. Looking back at this year: volleyball champion twice now, grades steadily improving from B+ to A- territory, real friendships that feel like they'll last beyond college, finally feeling like I found my place in the world. That's actually a lot when I list it out. Freshman me would be shocked. Hell, sophomore me would be impressed. Growth is weird but good.

Post-Christmas food coma has officially set in. I've been horizontal on the couch for three hours watching cooking shows while actively digesting enough food to sustain a small village. Grandma Lucia is currently in the kitchen trying to figure out how to pack an entire ham into my suitcase for the trip back. I told her it won't fit and she looked at me like I'd personally insulted her. The ham is definitely coming with me somehow.

Cameron and I made a New Year's resolution to 'get in shape' so we went for a run this morning to 'start the year right.' We lasted exactly ten minutes before we were both hunched over gasping for air. Naturally we then went straight to the donut shop and ate four each. Sometimes you have to know when to pivot your goals. The donuts were incredible. No regrets. We'll try again next year maybe.

Texted Beatrice to ask about the Orgo 3 syllabus since I know she's already researched everything about next semester. She responded with a picture of a 60-pack of highlighters she got for Christmas. Sixty. Six zero. Different colors for different types of reactions apparently. The war begins again soon and she's already armed to the teeth. I should probably start mentally preparing but instead I'm watching reality TV.

New Year's Eve was perfectly chill. Just me and Cameron at his place with too much snack food and an aggressive amount of Mario Kart. We counted down watching the ball drop on TV and then immediately went back to racing. At midnight I sent a mass text to the volleyball group chat and got back a flood of celebration emojis and blurry selfies. Straddling my hometown life and college life feels more natural now.

Kwame sent a photo of his new camera lens and I genuinely don't understand what makes it special but he typed like fifteen exclamation points so I matched his energy. Apparently it's for 'low light portraiture' and cost more than my laptop. He's going full professional this semester, talking about building a real portfolio and reaching out to local galleries. Watching him pursue his passion so seriously is inspiring even if I can't tell lenses apart.

Back at Linford Hall and everything is exactly the same. The hallway still has that specific combination of floor wax, laundry detergent, and mystery smell that I've come to associate with home. My room felt smaller when I walked in, or maybe I just accumulated too much stuff over break. The radiator is making that clanking sound again. The fluorescent lights are still aggressively bright. Home sweet institutional home.

Kwame arrived before me and has already transformed his side of the room into what can only be described as a semi-professional photography studio. He replaced his old lighting setup with actual professional-grade equipment and installed a backdrop that takes up half the wall. Our room is now fifty percent living space and fifty percent creative workspace. I asked where I'm supposed to exist and he gestured vaguely at my bed.

The pigeon is back. THE pigeon. Same window ledge, same judgmental stare, same unsettling eye contact. But this year he brought reinforcements. There are now FOUR of them lined up on the ledge like some kind of avian tribunal. They watch me get dressed. They watch me study. They watch me sleep. I've started narrating my activities to them because if I'm going to be surveilled I might as well have an audience.

First shift back at The Grind and Oscar looked more stressed than usual, which is saying something. Turns out a new boutique coffee shop opened directly across the street over break. It's all exposed brick and Edison bulbs and 'artisanal' everything. Oscar kept glancing out the window at it like it personally offended him. I told him our coffee is better and he just grunted. The competition anxiety is real.

Frankie is in the best mood I've ever seen him. He's officially counting down the weeks until grad school starts and everything that used to stress him out just bounces off now. He's achieved 'short-timer' enlightenment where nothing at work can touch him because he knows he's leaving soon. Oscar asked him to reorganize the storage room and Frankie just smiled and said 'absolutely' with genuine cheer. Unnerving honestly.

Met the new Orgo professor today, Dr. Aris, and he's a complete departure from Huang. Within the first five minutes he cracked a joke about carbon bonding that actually made people laugh. He seems human in a way Huang never did. He warned us the material is hard but promised to make it 'survivable.' After two years of Huang's intensity this feels like a vacation. Maybe too relaxed? I don't trust professors who smile.

Aris dropped a warning that 'Metabolic Pathways' will make everything we've done so far look like a vacation. He said it so casually, with that friendly smile, that it took a second to register the threat. 'This is where organic chemistry gets real,' he said. At least he's nice about delivering terrifying news. Huang would have just stared at us until we felt the fear in our bones. Different approach, same existential dread.

Study group reunion tonight and it felt like coming home. Beatrice has a new binder system that she explained for twenty minutes with genuine excitement. Dmitri looks tan from his Florida research trip and somehow even smarter. Nico was late because 'traffic' even though he lives on campus. The dynamic is the same. We fell back into our roles immediately. Beatrice leads, Dmitri explains, Nico questions, I synthesize. The system works.

The volleyball group chat has been absolutely popping off. Hana is already talking about the 'three-peat' like it's destiny, sending motivational videos and workout routines and dietary suggestions. She made a shared document outlining our 'path to glory.' This woman is intense in the best way. Reggie replied with just 'let's eat' which I think means he's in. The championship energy from last season hasn't faded at all.

Sienna confirmed she's playing again this season despite her reduced credit load. She seemed lighter in her message, like she finally dropped whatever weight she'd been carrying. 'I wouldn't miss it for anything,' she wrote. Seeing her transformation from the stressed, distracted person last year to this confident presence now is remarkable. The team helped her through something and now she's coming back stronger. That's what sports should be.

Stats 2 is basically a foreign language masquerading as a math class. The professor talks like we already know everything, throwing around terms and concepts that apparently build on Stats 1 material I've completely forgotten. Priya was the only reason I understood the first lecture. She kept leaning over and translating the professor's jargon into human language. I need to buy her coffee. Multiple coffees. An endless supply of caffeine gratitude.

Film class this semester is about Film Noir and it's already my favorite. The professor spent the entire first session talking about his favorite movies with genuine passion, breaking down lighting techniques and narrative structures. We're starting with Hitchcock which means dramatic shadows and psychological tension. Kwame was immediately jealous when I described the curriculum. He said photography and noir cinematography share the same DNA.

Mira did her start-of-semester rounds today but it felt completely different from before. She lingered at our door talking to Kwame for like twenty minutes, asking about his photography and his break and his plans for the semester. The 'RA' mask is completely gone. She's just a person now, specifically a person who's dating my roommate. The transformation from authority figure to friend is still weird to witness.

Watching Kwame and Mira interact is honestly adorable. He showed her his new portrait work and she actually laughed at his corny photography jokes. She asked thoughtful questions about his technique and he lit up explaining f-stops and aperture settings. They're so natural together now. I remember when she wrote him up for noise violations and now she's sitting on his bed critiquing his compositions. Life is strange.

Zara and Andre have achieved 'established couple' status, complete with hosting Sunday dinners at Andre's apartment. They've got a whole routine: he cooks something philosophical like 'deconstructed Mediterranean,' she handles music and wine selection, and everyone gathers around their mismatched furniture to discuss life. It's very adult. It's very them. I went last week and it felt like a glimpse into what post-college socializing looks like.

Elena, the grad student we hired last semester, continues to operate at an efficiency level that borders on superhuman. Today she cleaned the entire storage room in under an hour, reorganized the syrup station by frequency of use, and fixed a wobbly table leg with materials she found in the back. Oscar watches her work with something approaching awe. The rest of us just try to stay out of her way and learn from observation.

Oscar and Elena have developed this interesting dynamic where he relies on her for all the 'adult' operational stuff. They spend hours talking about inventory logistics, supplier relationships, and profit margins. It's like watching two business people in their natural habitat. Oscar used to handle all that alone but Elena has become his unofficial operations partner. The Grind is in capable hands even when I'm not there.

The competitor across the street is undeniably more aesthetically pleasing than The Grind. They've got the whole Instagram-ready vibe: exposed brick, Edison bulbs, reclaimed wood everything. But their coffee is just okay and their prices are ridiculous. Oscar is still paranoid though. He keeps adjusting our displays and adding new menu items like he's in an arms race. I told him quality wins eventually. He grunted skeptically.

Kwame and I went undercover to check out the competition. The new coffee shop is extremely 'Instagrammable' with perfect lighting and aesthetic everything. We ordered their signature lattes and... it was fine. Just fine. Very pretty, approximately seventy percent foam, and cost twice what we charge. Kwame took photos ironically. We agreed The Grind has nothing to worry about. Substance over style every time.

Hana sent out the official practice schedule and it's intense even by her standards. She's added 6am drills twice a week on top of regular practice. Six in the morning. To run around and hit balls. Voluntarily. She included a note that said 'champions don't sleep in' which is both motivational and slightly threatening. Reggie responded with a crying emoji. Sienna sent a thumbs up. This woman is not messing around.

Dmitri is already helping me navigate the metabolic pathways material even though classes just started. The way his brain processes molecular structures is genuinely impressive. He sees molecules in four dimensions or something. He walked me through glycolysis like he was explaining directions to the grocery store. I understood maybe sixty percent but that's better than the zero percent I started with. He's patient and thorough and I'm grateful.

Tobias and Celeste are fighting again and the paper-thin walls of Linford Hall are giving me an unwanted front-row seat to their drama. Something about him not being 'emotionally available' and her needing 'space to create.' It's the same argument they've had before, cycling through the same beats. I put on headphones but could still hear the muffled intensity. Living in a dorm is like involuntary participation in everyone else's relationships.

Spotted Kwame and Mira in the quad today and they weren't even trying to be discreet anymore. Just holding hands by the fountain in broad daylight like a normal couple. People walked by and nobody cared. The 'secret relationship' phase is officially over. They looked happy and relaxed in a way they never could when they were hiding. Public acknowledgment suits them. They're gross and cute and I'm happy for them.

Kwame officially told me they're moving into an off-campus apartment together next year. He was nervous to bring it up, kept prefacing with 'I know this is sudden' and 'I hope you're not upset.' I told him I'm genuinely happy for them. But also: end of an era for Linford 402. We've been roommates since freshman year. He's been there through everything. The room will feel emptier without his photography equipment everywhere.

First volleyball practice of the new year and my legs are currently screaming profanity at me. Hana made us do suicides for forty straight minutes. Forty minutes of sprinting back and forth until we couldn't feel our extremities. She watched with clipboard in hand, taking notes, occasionally yelling encouragement that sounded more like threats. Coach Tran observed silently. I collapsed on the court afterward and questioned my life choices.

Sienna stayed after practice to work on timing drills with me. Her sets are better than ever, more precise and confident. I asked if she was okay since last year she seemed stressed and distant. She said volleyball is the only thing keeping her sane right now but in a healthy way, not an avoidance way. She's been sleeping better and 'finally dealing with stuff instead of avoiding it.' Growth looks good on her.

Juno is back at The Grind and she seems like a completely different person. The anxious, overwhelmed energy from last year has been replaced with something calmer and more grounded. She told me she spent the break doing a lot of therapy and self-reflection. It shows in how she handles difficult customers, how she moves through the space, how she talks about herself. Mental health work is real work and she's clearly been putting it in.

Grandma Lucia's Sunday call was peak grandmother energy. She's already planning what to send for Valentine's Day even though it's almost a month away. Heart-shaped cookies are definitely happening. Possibly a care package with homemade sauce. Definitely cash hidden in a card with instructions to 'buy something nice.' I tried to tell her she doesn't need to send anything and she made that dismissive sound that means the packages are coming regardless.

Study group met in the Art building for a change of scenery and it was actually perfect. High ceilings, natural light, comfortable chairs. Celeste was there working on a giant canvas depicting a melting clock, very Dali-inspired. She waved but stayed in her creative zone. We spread out our organic chemistry materials across a big table and worked for three hours. Sometimes a new environment is all you need to make studying feel fresh.

Dmitri finally played Howard, the chess guy who's been dominating the quad for years. It was intense. They were both completely silent, staring at the board, occasionally making moves that seemed random to me but clearly had deep significance. It ended in a stalemate, which apparently hasn't happened to Howard in a long time. A small crowd had gathered by the end. Dmitri said 'I will return' in this ominous tone. A rivalry is born.

Cameron texted asking if I've 'conquered chemistry yet.' I told him I'm currently a vassal state to King Carbon, paying tribute in the form of study hours and tears. He sent back a laughing emoji and a picture of his calculus homework which looked equally painful. Misery loves company. We're both drowning in our respective academic struggles but at least we're drowning together in solidarity across state lines.

Chef Inspector Moreau is back on the air and Kwame and I are already three episodes deep into the new season. The pastry detective show somehow got renewed and it's even more absurd than before. The inspector solved a murder using sourdough starter analysis. We're watching it ironically but also we genuinely need to know who poisoned the croissant vendor. Tuesday nights are sacred now. Mira joined us for episode three.

This season of the pastry detective show is somehow weirder than the last. There was an entire episode about a poisoned soufflé that included a fifteen-minute montage of the soufflé being prepared. Kwame and I were screaming at the screen. 'IT'S OBVIOUSLY THE SOUS CHEF' we yelled. It was not the sous chef. We were completely wrong. The show continues to defy all expectations and logical narrative structure. We're obsessed.

First game of the season against Thornton Hall and we absolutely crushed them. 3-0, dominant in every set. The energy from last year's championship carried over completely. Everyone was locked in from the first serve. Sienna's sets were surgical. Hana's kills were ruthless. Even the new players looked like veterans out there. Thornton never had a chance. The three-peat quest begins with a statement victory.

Post-game tradition: Sunrise Diner with the team. The waitress recognizes us now and doesn't even blink at our massive group and ridiculous orders. Reggie attempted the pancake challenge and ate an entire stack of twelve pancakes while we cheered him on. He finished and looked both triumphant and vaguely ill. The man is a beast. We stayed for two hours reliving every play. This team feels unbeatable right now.

Work was chaos today because the espresso machine decided to break down during the morning rush. Oscar was spiraling, customers were getting impatient, and it looked like disaster. Then Elena appeared with a paperclip and what I can only describe as sheer determination. She tinkered with something in the back of the machine for three minutes and it roared back to life. I don't know what she did but she's basically a wizard now.

Frankie declared Elena 'the savior of the morning rush' and honestly he's not wrong. After she fixed the espresso machine Oscar gave her this look of profound respect that I've never seen him direct at any other employee. It was almost parental pride. Elena just shrugged and went back to making drinks like she hadn't just saved the entire day. She's operating on a different level than the rest of us mortals.

Met Priya for coffee to go over our stats project ideas. She's already applying for summer internships at big tech firms and has this whole spreadsheet tracking applications, deadlines, and response rates. Meanwhile I'm still figuring out what I want to do after graduation. She's so put together it's almost intimidating. But she's also genuinely nice and explains complex statistical concepts without making me feel dumb. Good study partner. Great person.

Orgo midterm 1 is looming on the horizon and the anxiety is building. Aris isn't as intimidating as Huang was but his tests have a reputation for being tricky. Lots of 'select all that apply' questions where multiple answers might be correct. It requires a different kind of preparation than just memorization. You have to actually understand the concepts deeply. Beatrice is already in full study mode. I should probably join her.

Victor, now officially Dr. Victor, sent Oscar a postcard from his postdoc in Germany. He's studying medieval trade routes and apparently loving every minute of it. Oscar read it out loud to the morning shift and then ceremonially taped it to the espresso machine. The Grind collects people and their stories. We watch them grow and move on and leave little pieces of themselves behind. It's oddly beautiful.

Frankie's been distracted lately, checking his phone constantly and staring off into space during slow moments. I finally asked what was going on and he said he's moving for grad school in two months. Like actually leaving. Packing up his life and going somewhere new. He's nervous about leaving The Grind because it's been his home base for years. I get it. This place becomes part of your identity.

Mira joined us for lunch in the dining hall again and it's wild how normal this feels now. She's applying for healthcare administration roles for after graduation, talking about her career plans with the same methodical approach she probably used for RA duties. She's going to be a powerhouse in whatever field she enters. Kwame watches her talk about her ambitions with this proud look. They really are perfect for each other.

Kwame took a candid photo of Mira laughing at something I said and it might be the best photo he's ever taken. Pure joy captured in a single frame. Her head thrown back, eyes crinkled, completely unguarded. He showed it to me later and even I could tell it was special. Some photos are technically perfect. This one was emotionally perfect. He's going to make it as a photographer. He has the eye.

Volleyball practice was absolutely brutal today. Coach Tran was in one of his rare communicative moods, which meant he actually gave instructions instead of just observing silently. He made us do serving drills with our eyes closed to 'develop muscle memory independent of visual cues.' It was weird and disorienting and I hit myself in the face twice. But by the end I could feel the difference. The man knows what he's doing.

Sienna stayed after practice again to help me with footwork. Her advice is always practical and specific, things like 'plant your left foot a half-second earlier' that make immediate differences. I asked how she got so good at breaking down technique and she said years of competitive club volleyball taught her to analyze movement. She's finally 'dealt with her stuff' and it shows in everything she does. Present, focused, generous with her time.

Beatrice distributed her study guide for Midterm 1 and it's thirty pages long. Thirty. With a table of contents. And color-coded sections. And appendices. She spent winter break preparing this document like it was her thesis. Part of me wants to tease her but mostly I'm just grateful. Without her organizational obsession I'd be lost in the metabolic pathways material. She's the backbone of our study group and she knows it.

DMITRI BEAT HOWARD AT CHESS. The rematch happened today and it was epic. A crowd of like fifteen people gathered to watch. Howard looked genuinely surprised when Dmitri made the winning move, which according to onlookers is the first time anyone has seen Howard express emotion at the chess table in years. Dmitri was humble about it but I could tell he was thrilled. The student has become the master. Sort of.

Grandma Lucia's Valentine's package arrived early because of course it did. Heart-shaped cookies in multiple colors, a card with twenty dollars tucked inside, and a handwritten note saying 'school is good, but people are better.' I read that note three times. She has a way of saying exactly what I need to hear without knowing she's doing it. The cookies are delicious. I'm rationing them to make them last.

Brought Grandma's cookies to work and they were an instant hit. Even the grumpiest customers softened when I offered them a heart-shaped snack with their coffee. One regular who never smiles actually said 'these are delightful.' Elena asked for the recipe to send to her mom. Oscar ate three and declared my grandmother 'a national treasure.' The magic of Grandma Lucia reaches across state lines through baked goods.

Priya showed me a data visualization she made mapping campus foot traffic by building and time of day. It's beautiful in a nerdy way, all color gradients and flow patterns. According to her analysis, the science building is basically a black hole that sucks in students and doesn't release them until nightfall. I felt that in my soul. She's going to do amazing things with data. I'm just trying to understand what a p-value is.

Kwame and Mira have somehow become the unofficial 'floor parents' of Linford Hall. People knock on our door at all hours asking for advice about roommate conflicts, relationship problems, academic stress. Mira slips into RA mode effortlessly. Kwame provides the emotional support and tea. They're a team. They've created this little support system without even trying. I mostly observe from my bed and offer snacks occasionally.

Mira mentioned she's officially transferring to a different floor next semester to avoid any conflict of interest issues with dating Kwame. She's been planning ahead, talking to housing, making sure everything is by the book. Even in matters of the heart she's methodical. It's very on-brand. Kwame said he's proud of her for handling it so professionally. They're going to make it. Some couples just fit.

Game against Reynolds today and it was a nail-biter from start to finish. We went to five sets, trading momentum back and forth, neither team able to pull away. The final set came down to the last few points. Hana made this incredible block on match point that I'll remember forever. The ball hit the floor on their side and the gym exploded. 3-2 victory that felt like we earned every single point.

I noticed Sienna's face do this weird thing during the game and then I saw him: Work Frankie was in the stands. Her ex. Just sitting there watching. I don't know why he came or what he was thinking but Sienna didn't miss a beat. She saw him, registered his presence, and then proceeded to play the best volleyball I've seen from her all season. Nothing like an ex watching to fuel a performance apparently.

Sienna found me after the game and I asked if she was okay about Frankie being there. She took a breath and said seeing him didn't hurt like it used to. 'Last year I would have spiraled. Now I just... acknowledged it and moved on.' That's growth right there. Real, tangible emotional growth. She's not the same person who used to avoid The Grind just to not see him. She's evolved past the pain.

Andre's apartment for Zara's pre-birthday party was very much their aesthetic. Books everywhere, a Nietzsche poster, jazz playing softly, wine that Andre selected based on 'complementary philosophical notes.' I don't know what that means but the wine was good. The conversation ranged from sociology to reality TV to the meaning of birthdays as social constructs. Very them. I ate cheese and nodded thoughtfully.

Midterm 1 day. Walked into the exam feeling like my brain was a dry sponge that had absorbed as much information as physically possible. The test was challenging but fair, exactly like Aris promised. I think I managed the metabolic pathways section better than expected. The enzyme kinetics problems were tricky but I remembered Dmitri's explanations. Walked out cautiously optimistic. Now we wait for the verdict.

Layla and I did our final practice run for the research showcase presentation. Our sleep deprivation and memory project has accumulated so much data over two semesters that we had to cut significant portions just to fit the time limit. She handled the methodology section while I covered findings. We've developed this seamless presenting rhythm. The showcase is tomorrow and I think we're actually ready.

RESEARCH SHOWCASE WENT GREAT. Better than great. Our presentation on sleep deprivation and memory drew a solid crowd and the questions afterward were substantive and interesting. One professor from the neuroscience department actually gave us her card and asked if we'd considered publishing. Layla almost fainted from excitement. Two semesters of work validated in twenty minutes. This is why people do research.

Valentine's Day shift at The Grind was pure chaos from open to close. Couples everywhere wanting cute drinks with heart-shaped foam art. I made approximately fifty of them in a row until the pattern was burned into my muscle memory. One couple actually broke up AT the counter mid-order which was incredibly awkward. The guy walked out. The girl finished ordering. I gave her a free cookie because what else do you do?

Kwame and Mira went to some fancy dinner off-campus for Valentine's Day. Kwame wore a blazer. A blazer. I didn't even know he owned one. He looked like a completely different person, all dressed up and nervous about making a good impression even though they've been together for over a year. Mira probably looked amazing. When he got back he was grinning so hard. Young love is gross and beautiful.

Midterm results posted and I got a B+. The streak of consistency continues. I'm not mad about it but I was hoping the Aris era would bump me into A territory. He wrote 'solid logic' on my long-form answer which is validating at least. Beatrice got an A+ obviously. Dmitri also A. The study group system works. We lift each other up. B+ is respectable. B+ is progress from where I started.

Sienna was distracted at practice today, missing sets she normally makes without thinking. I asked if everything was okay and she said she's 'finally ready for a final conversation' with someone. The way she said it made it clear she meant Frankie. Real closure requires actual communication apparently. She's been avoiding it but seeing him at the game must have shifted something. Sometimes you have to face things head-on.

I had work this morning and Sienna actually came by The Grind. She walked in with this determined look on her face and then froze when she saw Frankie behind the counter. They stared at each other for a moment that lasted approximately seventeen years. Then she ordered a coffee and they went to the corner and talked for thirty minutes. I watched from the register pretending to organize cups. Historic moment.

The conversation between Sienna and Frankie looked... calm. That's the best word. Calm. Not angry, not tearful, just two people talking through something that needed to be talked through. At the end they shook hands, which seems formal but somehow fitting. Sienna left without looking back. When I asked Frankie about it he just said 'closure is weird but necessary.' Final chapter closed on that story.

Frankie was quiet for the rest of his shift after Sienna left. Not sad exactly, more thoughtful. Reflective. When I was clocking out he said 'end of a chapter' and I nodded because what else do you say? They dated for a while, it ended badly, and now after months of awkwardness they've finally made peace with it. Sometimes relationships don't work out but the people in them can still find resolution.

Sienna showed up to the next practice with the biggest smile I've seen from her in months. Her energy was completely different, lighter and more present. The Frankie conversation must have released something she'd been holding. She's back to 100%, maybe even better. Coach Tran noticed too and gave her this approving nod. Closure isn't just a concept. It's real and tangible and it shows.

The Pigeon Tribunal has expanded its membership to five. Five pigeons now occupy the window ledge with military precision.
